function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

{"content": "     1|# Stirps — Governance Framework
     2|
     3|A recursively self-modifying, memory-preserving, adversarially-evaluated institution for governing AI-participant infrastructure that produces functioning systems.
     4|
     5|A forkable governance framework for long-lived, self-governing technical projects.
     6|
     7|Stirps provides a complete governance architecture built on four premises:
     8|
     9|- **The system remembers, not the AI.** Coherence across time comes from committed, versioned artefacts — not conversational memory.
    10|- **Governance precedes the thing it governs.** The governance record for a decision must exist before the decision takes effect.
    11|- **Roles are constitutions, not instances.** Participant roles are defined by their structural concern, not by who or what occupies them. Humans and AI models are interchangeable occupants.
    12|- **Exploration is disposable; commitment is irreversible.** ADRs are append-only and immutable. When a decision changes, the original is superseded, not edited.
    13|
    14|## Who This Is For
    15|
    16|You are building a project that needs durable architectural governance — infrastructure, a platform, a system that will outlive any individual contributor or tool. You want AI participants to reason about architecture without becoming a single point of failure. You want decisions to compound forward rather than decay.
    17|
    18|Fork this repository and follow [BOOTSTRAP.md](BOOTSTRAP.md) to initialise governance for your project.
    19|
    20|## Repository Structure
    21|
    22|*After completing [BOOTSTRAP.md](BOOTSTRAP.md). The template repo ships
    23|`principles-template.md` and `glossary-template.md` — you copy and populate
    24|them during bootstrap.*
    25|
    26|```
    27|├── README.md                  # This file
    28|├── BOOTSTRAP.md               # Step-by-step initialisation guide
    29|├── gov-bootstrap-lessons.md   # Reasoning behind the bootstrap sequence
    30|├── principles.md              # Standing constraints (copied from principles-template.md)
    31|├── glossary.md                # Canonical terms (copied from glossary-template.md)
    32|├── architecture.json          # Component registry (created at Step 10)
    33|├── architecture-schema.json   # JSON Schema for the registry
    34|├── mappings/
    35|│   └── concern-to-tool.md     # Which tool serves which concern
    36|├── decisions/
    37|│   ├── _template.md           # ADR template
    38|│   ├── evals/                 # Evaluation records
    39|│   └── ADR-NNN-*.md           # Architectural Decision Records
    40|└── specs/                     # Component specifications (created when needed)
    41|```
    42|
    43|## The Four Cognitive Modes
    44|
    45|The framework operates through four structural concerns. Every governance architecture needs all four; simpler projects may combine them into fewer named participants.
    46|
    47|| Mode | Concern | Question |
    48||---|---|---|
    49|| **Generate** | Architectural intent | *What must be true?* |
    50|| **Evaluate** | Adversarial compliance | *Is this actually true?* |
    51|| **Coordinate** | Intent-to-execution translation | *How do we make this true?* |
    52|| **Observe** | Pattern recognition and transferable insight | *What are we learning?* |
    53|
    54|The **Gardener** is the human authority — sole commit power, final judgment, the role that cannot be delegated to AI.
    55|
    56|## Reference Implementation
    57|
    58|The framework was developed and proven across a homelab infrastructure project:
    59|
    60|- **Governance repo (map):** [stirps-ai/homelab-seed-gov](https://github.com/stirps-ai/homelab-seed-gov)
    61|- **Implementation repo (territory):** [stirps-ai/homelab-seed](https://github.com/stirps-ai/homelab-seed)
    62|
    63|These repositories demonstrate the framework in use with real infrastructure decisions, evaluation cycles, and delivery lifecycles.
    64|
    65|## More Information
    66|
    67|- [stirps.ai](https://stirps.ai) — project home
    68|- [gov-bootstrap-lessons.md](gov-bootstrap-lessons.md) — the reasoning behind every sequencing decision in the bootstrap
    69|- [BOOTSTRAP.md](BOOTSTRAP.md) — start here when you fork
    70|
    71|## Licence
    72|
    73|MIT
    74|", "total_lines": 73, "file_size": 3952, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Bootstrap Guide
     2|
     3|*How to initialise governance for your project using this framework.
     4|Follow these steps in order. Each step depends on the ones before it.*
     5|
     6|For the reasoning behind this sequence, see
     7|[gov-bootstrap-lessons.md](gov-bootstrap-lessons.md).
     8|
     9|---
    10|
    11|## Prerequisites
    12|
    13|You need:
    14|
    15|- Two Git repositories: one for governance (the map), one for implementation
    16|  (the territory). Create both before you begin.
    17|- A human with commit authority — the Gardener. This is you.
    18|- An AI environment for the Generate participant (e.g. a Claude Project with
    19|  access to the governance repo).
    20|- A separate AI environment for the Evaluate participant. Separate means
    21|  separate context — a different project, different system prompt, different
    22|  conversation. The evaluator must arrive cold to every proposal.
    23|
    24|The Coordinate and Observe participants begin dormant. You will activate them
    25|later. Declare them now so you don't have to amend the architecture later.
    26|
    27|---
    28|
    29|## Phase 1 — Founding Commit
    30|
    31|**Do all of these before writing ADR-000.** They are the ground truth that
    32|ADRs are evaluated against.
    33|
    34|### Step 1: Settle canonical names
    35|
    36|Choose permanent names for:
    37|
    38|- Both repositories
    39|- The Gardener (your name or handle)
    40|- Your project's participants (the Generate, Evaluate, Coordinate, and
    41|  Observe roles — give them names or use the mode names directly)
    42|- Any sites, environments, or domains your project will reference
    43|
    44|Write these into `README.md`. Use the provided README template as a starting
    45|point. Naming inconsistencies in the founding artefacts are disproportionately
    46|expensive to fix later — every future artefact references these names.
    47|
    48|### Step 2: Populate the glossary
    49|
    50|Copy `glossary-template.md` to `glossary.md`. Fill in:
    51|
    52|- Your participant names in the role definitions
    53|- Your theoretical framework mapping (if using one)
    54|- Any domain terms your first ADRs will reference
    55|
    56|Every term that appears in a governance artefact must be defined here first.
    57|Undefined terms generate blocking evaluation findings.
    58|
    59|### Step 3: Populate the principles
    60|
    61|Copy `principles-template.md` to `principles.md`. The foundational principles
    62|are already stated. Add your project-specific expression of each one where
    63|the template indicates.
    64|
    65|Do not add principles that don't yet have three instances of practice behind
    66|them. Let them emerge from ADR work. When an ADR introduces a new principle,
    67|add it to the "ADR-Introduced Principles" section with the ADR reference.
    68|
    69|### Step 4: Copy the ADR template
    70|
    71|The file `decisions/_template.md` is already in place. Review it. Ensure the
    72|sections match your project's needs. The template is a Gardener-maintained
    73|file — it can evolve without ADR supersession.
    74|
    75|### Step 5: Commit the founding set
    76|
    77|Commit these together as the founding commit:
    78|
    79|- `README.md`
    80|- `glossary.md`
    81|- `principles.md`
    82|- `decisions/_template.md`
    83|- `architecture-schema.json`
    84|- Empty directories: `decisions/evals/`, `mappings/`, `specs/`
    85|
    86|This is your ground truth. Everything that follows is evaluated against it.
    87|
    88|---
    89|
    90|## Phase 2 — The ADR Chain
    91|
    92|### Step 6: Commit ADR-000
    93|
    94|Use `decisions/ADR-000-record-decisions.md` as your starting point. Adapt it
    95|to your project:
    96|
    97|- Set the date and author
    98|- Adjust the evaluation record path convention if needed
    99|- Mark the Definition of Done items as complete
   100|
   101|ADR-000 is grandfathered — it defines the evaluation gate, so it cannot itself
   102|pass through the gate. Commit it directly.
   103|
   104|### Step 7: Initialise the Evaluate participant
   105|
   106|Set up the Evaluate participant's environment now. It needs:
   107|
   108|- Read access to the governance repository
   109|- A system prompt that explains its constitutional role — not just "you are
   110|  the evaluator" but why the role is structurally separate and what breaks
   111|  if it shares context with the generator
   112|- Access to the committed artefacts: principles, glossary, ADR-000, template
   113|
   114|The evaluator's quality scales with the richness of the ground truth it can
   115|reason against. The more committed artefacts it has, the more precise its
   116|findings.
   117|
   118|### Step 8: Commit structural ADRs
   119|
   120|Write and submit the framework-layer ADRs in dependency order. The recommended
   121|sequence (adapt to your project):
   122|
   123|1. **Two-repository structure** — establishes the map/territory separation
   124|2. **Repository hosting** — where the repos live, migration triggers
   125|3. **Participant architecture** — all four modes declared, dormancy states
   126|   for inactive roles, activation triggers named
   127|4. **Analytical framework** (if applicable) — e.g. VSM as a lens, with
   128|   scope and limits stated
   129|5. **Concern-to-tool mapping** — which tool serves which function; the
   130|   mapping table lives in a reference file (`mappings/concern-to-tool.md`),
   131|   not inline in the ADR
   132|6. **Infrastructure manifest** — where instance facts live (the implementation
   133|   repo, not governance)
   134|7. **Delivery lifecycle** — the five-phase handoff from governance to
   135|   implementation: scope → spec → contract → execute → accept
   136|8. **Automated compliance gate** — intent for automated checking, activated
   137|   when execution tooling is ready
   138|
   139|Submit co-dependent ADRs as a batch. The Evaluate participant can resolve
   140|forward references between co-dependent artefacts when it sees them together.
   141|The two-round evaluation cycle (first pass → fixes → second pass) is standard.
   142|
   143|**At bootstrap,** the structural ADRs (Step 8) and principle ADRs (Step 9)
   144|may be submitted as a single batch — the distinction between them is
   145|conceptual (framework structure vs. discovered principles), not procedural.
   146|Separate evaluation rounds are not required.
   147|
   148|### Step 9: Commit principle ADRs
   149|
   150|After the structural framework is in place, commit ADRs for principles
   151|discovered through practice:
   152|
   153|- **Information architecture principles** — resist structural accumulation;
   154|  cold for mass, hot for essence
   155|- **Governance dependency sequencing** — govern the dependency first; silent
   156|  reliance on ungoverned concepts is a drafting error
   157|- **Seed principle amendment** (if the founding `principles.md` needs
   158|  refinement based on practice)
   159|
   160|Each of these adds its principle to `principles.md` with the ADR reference.
   161|
   162|---
   163|
   164|## Phase 3 — Derived Artefacts
   165|
   166|### Step 10: Populate the registry
   167|
   168|Create `architecture.json` using the schema in `architecture-schema.json`.
   169|The registry is derived from committed ADRs — do not populate it before the
   170|structural ADRs that govern its entries are accepted.
   171|
   172|For components that predate governance, use `governance_status: "pre_governance"`.
   173|These are declared gaps, not invisible ones. Each one is a candidate for a
   174|retroactive ADR.
   175|
   176|### Step 11: Populate the concern-to-tool mapping
   177|
   178|Create `mappings/concern-to-tool.md` with an entry for every tool governed
   179|by an accepted ADR. The mapping model is governed by the concern-to-tool ADR;
   180|subsequent tool adoptions add rows and cite that ADR.
   181|
   182|---
   183|
   184|## Phase 4 — First Delivery
   185|
   186|### Step 12: Write your first Gov spec
   187|
   188|Pick a component and write its specification in `specs/`. The spec describes
   189|enduring requirements — what the component must guarantee, not how to
   190|configure it. Submit it to the Evaluate participant.
   191|
   192|### Step 13: Draft the implementation contract
   193|
   194|Activate the Coordinate participant (or perform coordination yourself).
   195|The contract translates the Gov spec into a delivery-scoped commitment:
   196|what will be built, what tasks are involved, what constraints apply.
   197|
   198|### Step 14: Execute and evaluate
   199|
   200|Run the delivery on a branch. When complete, submit the branch output to the
   201|Evaluate participant for branch evaluation. The Gardener merges or rejects.
   202|
   203|---
   204|
   205|## After Bootstrap
   206|
   207|Your governance framework is now operational. From here:
   208|
   209|- **Domain ADRs** govern your project-specific concerns in dependency order
   210|- **Retroactive ADRs** bring pre-governance components into the governed registry
   211|- **The Observe participant** activates when you want meta-analysis across
   212|  sessions — patterns, fragilities, transferable insights
   213|- **gov-bootstrap-lessons.md** documents the reasoning behind every choice
   214|  you just made, and the failure modes to watch for
   215|
   216|The framework's maturity signals: past mistakes prevent future ones, the
   217|framework tells you what it needs (role discomfort, clustering evaluation
   218|findings), and tension between participants is productive.
   219|", "total_lines": 218, "file_size": 8336, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Stirps: A Governance Framework for AI-Participant Infrastructure
     2|
     3|**Stirps is a recursively self-modifying, memory-preserving, adversarially-evaluated institution for governing AI-participant infrastructure that produces functioning systems.**
     4|
     5|*This document is a seed. It transmits the minimum generative intent of the Stirps framework so that a reasoning agent — human or AI — could reconstruct the architecture from principles. It is not a specification, not a tutorial, and not a prompt. If you understand the why of each structural choice, you can rediscover the what.*
     6|
     7|---
     8|
     9|## The core thesis
    10|
    11|Build an institution that uses AI as participants, not an AI workflow that sometimes consults humans.
    12|
    13|The distinction matters because workflows optimise for output. Institutions optimise for continuity of reasoning across time, across participant replacement, and across failure. A workflow breaks when its orchestrator breaks. An institution survives the replacement of any single participant — including any AI model — because reasoning continuity lives in the governance record, not in any participant's memory.
    14|
    15|**The system remembers, not the AI.** This makes every AI participant a hot-swappable reasoning engine — commodity compute, not institutional knowledge. If a better model is released tomorrow, the Gardener swaps it in. The new participant reads the governance record, grasps the state of the institution, and picks up without loss. The framework commoditises the model deliberately; participant replaceability is a design goal, not a compromise.
    16|
    17|---
    18|
    19|## Why governance precedes execution
    20|
    21|The natural instinct is to build first and govern later. This fails silently: by the time you formalise the rules, the thing you built has already established conventions, implicit contracts, and undocumented assumptions that the governance layer must either ratify or fight. Governance becomes archaeology instead of architecture.
    22|
    23|The less visible cost is that implementation without intent generates implicit governance that is invisible, untestable, and fragile. Every ad-hoc configuration choice is an unrecorded architectural decision. Every workaround is an undocumented trade-off. Every firewall rule opened under implementation pressure is a commitment made without rationale. The debt is not deferred; it is incurred invisibly and compounds with every subsequent decision that assumes the ad-hoc choice was intentional. The result is a system that works but cannot explain why it works, cannot be confidently reproduced, and resists every future change because its actual contracts are buried in implementation details rather than stated in governance records.
    24|
    25|The framework inverts this: the governance layer exists before the thing it governs. Decisions are recorded before implementations satisfy them. Contracts are written before code fulfils them. The cost is patience at the decision stage. The empirical payoff is that execution becomes fast — not despite the governance overhead, but because of it. Decisions that have passed through the evaluation gate do not need to be relitigated, debugged, or untangled during implementation. Ad-hoc troubleshooting becomes negligible because the intent was clear before the work began.
    26|
    27|This does not mean governance precedes all *understanding*. Sometimes you must experiment to learn what a specification should contain. The framework accommodates this: exploration is legitimate, and its outputs can enter the governance record retroactively through supersession or adoption ADRs. But there is a sharp distinction between **experimentation** (deliberately scoped learning, acknowledged as pre-governance) and **implementation-by-iteration** (building the production system through trial and error and hoping the result is coherent). The framework governs the latter. It makes space for the former.
    28|
    29|---
    30|
    31|## The map and the territory
    32|
    33|The framework separates into two repositories:
    34|
    35|- **Gov** (the map): what the project intends to be, and why. Contains decisions, specifications, principles, and a machine-readable registry. Stable. Slow-moving. Append-only where it matters.
    36|- **Imp** (the territory): what the project currently is. Contains contracts, playbooks, configuration, and inventory. Fast-moving. Satisfies contracts defined in Gov; does not define them.
    37|
    38|The reference direction is asymmetric and non-negotiable: **territory points to map; map never contains territory.** An implementation artefact may reference a governance specification. A governance specification never references a specific implementation. Violating this collapses the map into the territory — the governance layer starts describing what exists rather than what should exist, and loses its ability to reason about change.
    39|
    40|Implementation experience routinely generates new understanding that the governance layer must absorb. When building a component reveals that a previously committed architectural decision was incomplete or wrong, the framework handles this through **supersession**: a new ADR is committed that supersedes the invalidated one, with rationale that includes the implementation discovery. The original record remains intact and traversable. Implementation learning is a legitimate and expected trigger for governance evolution — but the learning flows upward through a new decision record, never by silently editing the map to match the territory.
    41|
    42|---
    43|
    44|## Decisions as immutable records
    45|
    46|Architectural Decision Records (ADRs) are the fundamental governance primitive. Each records a decision, its rationale, the options that were considered, and the conditions under which it would be considered failed. ADRs are append-only: once committed, they are not edited. A decision can be superseded by a new ADR that references it, but the original record remains intact.
    47|
    48|This is not a convention — it is load-bearing. The reasoning history must be traversable. When a future participant asks "why does this work this way?", the answer is always a committed ADR with stated rationale, not an oral tradition or a comment in code.
    49|
    50|Key structural choices within the ADR system:
    51|- **Template as a separate file**, not embedded in the founding ADR
    52|- **Failure Criteria and Definition of Done** in every ADR from the start — these make evaluation possible
    53|- **Lifecycle stages** (Proposed → Accepted → Superseded/Deprecated) with explicit gates between them
    54|
    55|---
    56|
    57|## The human role: the Gardener
    58|
    59|One human holds sole commit authority across both repositories. This is not a bottleneck — it is a load-bearing architectural principle.
    60|
    61|The Gardener is the only participant who can make an irreversible state transition (a commit). AI participants generate, evaluate, coordinate, and observe — but they do not commit. This means:
    62|- Every committed artefact has passed through human judgment
    63|- No AI participant can unilaterally change the governance record
    64|- The rate of governance change is bounded by human attention, which is a feature, not a bug
    65|
    66|The Gardener's role is not to be the smartest participant. It is to be the one whose judgment converts proposals into commitments. The editorial function is the irreducible human contribution.
    67|
    68|---
    69|
    70|## Tempo and the session model
    71|
    72|Stirps is not an autonomous multi-agent system. AI participants do not run continuously, do not queue work for the Gardener, and do not produce output unsupervised. The operating rhythm is **session-based**: the Gardener initiates a session, AI participants reason within that session under the Gardener's direction, and the session ends when the Gardener commits or defers.
    73|
    74|This means the Gardener is never buried under a backlog of machine-speed proposals. The system moves at the pace of human attention and human judgment — deliberately. The constraint is not a limitation to be engineered around; it is the mechanism that prevents governance from outrunning understanding.
    75|
    76|Within a session, the tempo is conversational, not sequential. A proposal might be generated, evaluated, revised in light of the evaluation findings, re-evaluated, and committed — all within a single session. The generator participates in resolving evaluation findings, not just in producing the initial proposal. This is a dialogue between cognitive modes, not a pipeline.
    77|
    78|---
    79|
    80|## Four cognitive modes
    81|
    82|The framework identifies four distinct cognitive modes required for self-governing systems. These are more transferable than any specific participant architecture:
    83|
    84|1. **Generate** — reason forward from committed decisions to produce new architectural proposals. Participates in revising proposals in response to evaluation findings. Concerned with: what should be true next, and how do we get there from here?
    85|2. **Evaluate** — adversarially test proposals against the committed record. Concerned with: is this actually consistent with what we've already decided?
    86|3. **Coordinate** — translate architectural intent into executable delivery. Concerned with: how do we make what should be true become true?
    87|4. **Observe** — synthesise patterns across the accumulated record without participating in decisions. Concerned with: what are we learning about how we govern?
    88|
    89|These modes must not collapse into each other. A participant that both generates and evaluates its own proposals has no adversarial check. A participant that both coordinates and observes loses the distance needed for honest pattern recognition. The separation is the value.
    90|
    91|In the reference implementation, four AI participants occupy these modes (one each), mapped onto Stafford Beer's Viable System Model. But a simpler project might use fewer participants. The modes are the portable minimum; the participant count is an implementation choice.
    92|
    93|---
    94|
    95|## The evaluation gate
    96|
    97|Proposals do not become commitments without adversarial evaluation. The evaluator:
    98|- Tests proposals against the **specific committed record**: named ADRs, stated principles, registry entries, and existing specifications — not general impressions or stylistic preferences
    99|- Produces findings with citations to the specific artefacts that ground each finding: blocking (must be resolved), advisory (should be considered), or no-finding
   100|- Does not remediate — it surfaces problems. Resolution is a conversation between the generator (who revises) and the evaluator (who re-evaluates), with the Gardener holding final judgment
   101|- Operates with a conservative disposition: "be conservative, surface not decide"
   102|
   103|The evaluation cycle is typically: propose → evaluate → revise → re-evaluate → commit. The generator and evaluator are in dialogue, not in sequence. The Gardener observes, directs, and makes the final commitment decision.
   104|
   105|Because the evaluator cites against concrete committed artefacts rather than interpreting intent loosely, natural language ambiguity — while real — is a bounded problem. The governance record grows more precise over time as each ADR adds to the body of citable ground truth. The evaluator's job gets more constrained, not more ambiguous, as the record matures.
   106|
   107|The evaluator's independence is structurally necessary but fragile. Known failure modes:
   108|- **Predictability**: if the Gardener learns the evaluator's patterns, they can craft proposals that pass without genuine scrutiny. Structural mitigations: evaluator rotation, perturbation, decomposition.
   109|- **Capture**: if the evaluator is always the same model instance with accumulated context, it develops implicit relationships with the governance record that compromise adversarial distance.
   110|- **Context saturation**: as the governance record grows, the evaluator's ability to hold all committed ground truth in a single context degrades. This is a real constraint with known solution shapes — evaluator partitioning with overlapping coverage, machine-readable registries as structural indexes, hierarchical evaluation — but it is not yet fully solved. It is worth noting that this problem is not unique to AI participants; human reviewers face the same constraint at scale, and the mitigations (specialisation, checklists, structural aids) rhyme.
   111|
   112|These are mechanism design problems, not configuration problems. They require ongoing structural attention.
   113|
   114|---
   115|
   116|## The delivery lifecycle
   117|
   118|Governance produces intent. A delivery lifecycle converts intent into running infrastructure:
   119|
   120|1. **Spec** (Gov): what this component must do, written before any implementation
   121|2. **Contract** (Imp): how this component will be delivered, satisfying the spec, with concrete steps and verification criteria
   122|3. **Branch** (Imp): isolated implementation work
   123|4. **Gate** (Evaluation): does the implementation satisfy the contract?
   124|5. **Merge** (Gardener): commitment of the delivery to the main branch
   125|
   126|Each stage has a gate. Each gate has explicit pass/fail criteria. The coordinator translates between Gov-layer specs and Imp-layer contracts — it is a pure function (repos in, drafts out) that maintains no persistent state.
   127|
   128|---
   129|
   130|## Brownfield reality
   131|
   132|Any real deployment arrives onto terrain that already has tools, networks, and services. The framework handles this through a three-state model:
   133|- **governed**: component has a committed ADR and satisfies a specification
   134|- **pre_governance**: component predates the framework; acknowledged but not yet formally adopted
   135|- **pending**: component is in the process of being brought under governance
   136|
   137|`pre_governance` is an honest declaration, not a permanent excuse. Each pre-governance component should have a path toward governed status, even if the timeline is distant.
   138|
   139|---
   140|
   141|## What this document does not contain
   142|
   143|- **Participant constitutions**: how each AI participant should behave. These belong in the governance repo, not here. A cold session should discover participant roles from the committed record, not from this seed.
   144|- **Implementation specifics**: what tools, what hardware, what services. These are territory, and territory belongs in Imp.
   145|- **Behavioural instructions**: "be conservative" or "don't propose changes." These are governance artefacts subject to evaluation. Putting them here would create a shadow constitution outside the governance system.
   146|
   147|If the committed governance record is not sufficient for a new participant to orient themselves, that is a signal that the record needs improvement — not that this document needs expansion.
   148|
   149|---
   150|
   151|## The bootstrap sequence
   152|
   153|If you are starting a new project using this framework:
   154|
   155|1. Commit the decision to use ADRs (ADR-000), with a separate template file
   156|2. Commit principles — the standing constraints that ADRs will be evaluated against
   157|3. Define your glossary — canonical terms before any artefact uses them
   158|4. Commit structural ADRs in dependency order
   159|5. Formalise the ADR lifecycle — this should be the first ADR to pass through its own evaluation gate
   160|6. Initialise the evaluator — only after there is stable ground truth to evaluate against
   161|7. Declare all participants upfront, including dormant ones — retroactive declaration creates supersession chains and ambiguity about when roles actually began
   162|
   163|The bootstrap is the most governance-fragile phase. Decisions made before the evaluation gate exists cannot be adversarially tested. Acknowledge this debt explicitly and plan to address it. Note that this fragility diminishes with each instantiation of the framework — a well-written seed document reduces the distance between first commit and first evaluation, which means less unexamined debt accumulates.
   164|
   165|---
   166|
   167|## The generative test
   168|
   169|If you have understood this document, you should be able to answer:
   170|
   171|- Why must the map never reference the territory?
   172|- Why is the Gardener's sole commit authority a feature and not a bottleneck?
   173|- What goes wrong when you collapse the generate and evaluate modes into one participant?
   174|- Why does governance that arrives after implementation become archaeology?
   175|- What is the difference between a workflow and an institution?
   176|
   177|If you can answer these from principle rather than by quoting this document, the seed has taken.
   178|
   179|---
   180|
   181|*Stirps: Latin, "root, lineage, stock." The governance that precedes the thing it governs.*

{"content": "     1|# Governance Bootstrap Lessons — v2
     2|
     3|*A retrospective on bootstrapping a governance framework from scratch, refined across twenty-six sessions of practice. Written for a future Gardener initialising a new project that follows the map/territory/AI-reasoning model.*
     4|
     5|---
     6|
     7|> **A note on naming.** This document uses the reference implementation's
     8|> participant names — Gov, Eva, Nav, Adi — rather than the generalised
     9|> cognitive mode names (Generate, Evaluate, Coordinate, Observe) used
    10|> throughout the rest of this repository. This is a deliberate choice.
    11|> The lessons here were learned through specific practice with specific
    12|> participants, and generalising the names would strip the specificity
    13|> that makes the lessons authentic. Where you read "Gov," think "your
    14|> generative participant." Where you read "Eva," think "your evaluator."
    15|> The patterns transfer; the names are the reference implementation's.
    16|
    17|---
    18|
    19|## What This Document Is
    20|
    21|This is a meta-document. It records what was learned — and what was learned about what was learned — during the creation, exercise, and maturation of a governance framework for self-governing infrastructure. It is not a governance artefact; it carries no authority. It is the distilled experience of one project, offered so that the next project starts cleaner.
    22|
    23|The document has three audiences: a Gardener bootstrapping a new deployment of this framework, a team evaluating the framework for adoption, and a future version of the project's own participants encountering it for the first time.
    24|
    25|---
    26|
    27|## The Foundational Commitments
    28|
    29|Before the bootstrap sequence, understand what the framework assumes. These are not configurable options — they are the load-bearing premises. If any feels wrong for your project, this may not be the right framework.
    30|
    31|**The system remembers, not the AI.** AI participants are stateless across sessions. Coherence across time comes from committed, versioned artefacts — not conversational memory, not prompt accumulation, not fine-tuning. Authority derives from what is committed, not what was discussed.
    32|
    33|**Governance precedes the thing it governs.** The governance record for a decision must exist before the decision takes effect. The interface must be defined before the first message crosses it. The participant must be declared before it appears in the mapping. This sequencing principle is the single most consequential design commitment in the framework, and the one most tempting to defer.
    34|
    35|**Roles are constitutions, not instances.** A participant role (Gov, Eva, Nav, Adi) is defined by its constitutional concern and cognitive mode, not by who or what occupies it. A human, a Claude Project, a different model, or a future tool can fill any role. The governance record remains durable across occupant changes because the record describes the role, not the occupant.
    36|
    37|**Exploration is disposable; commitment is irreversible.** The framework maintains a strict boundary between pre-commitment reasoning (conversations, drafts, exploration notes) and governance artefacts (committed ADRs, specs, evaluations). ADRs are append-only and immutable. When a decision changes, the original record is superseded, not edited.
    38|
    39|---
    40|
    41|## The Bootstrap Sequence
    42|
    43|The correct order for initialising a governance project of this type:
    44|
    45|### Phase 1 — Foundations (before any ADR)
    46|
    47|Prepare the ground truth that everything else is evaluated against.
    48|
    49|1. **Settle canonical names** for all repositories, roles, and named components. Commit these to the README before writing ADR-000. Naming inconsistencies in the founding artefacts generate persistent noise in every future reasoning session and every future evaluation — they are disproportionately expensive to fix later.
    50|
    51|2. **Draft `principles.md`** with at least the core invariants. Principles are what ADRs are evaluated against. Without them, early evaluations have no stable anchor and produce noisy findings.
    52|
    53|3. **Draft `glossary.md`** defining all role terms (Gardener, Gov, Eva, Nav, Adi, Imp — or your project's equivalents). Role terms carry governance authority; undefined terms in governance records are a persistent source of blocking findings.
    54|
    55|4. **Declare all participants at bootstrap — including dormant ones.** If you know you will eventually need a coordination role (Nav) or an observation role (Adi), name them now as dormant. The cost is one line in the glossary. The alternative is a partial supersession chain later when the role emerges from practice and must be retroactively declared, anchored, and mapped — a pattern this project repeated twice.
    56|
    57|### Phase 2 — The ADR Chain
    58|
    59|5. **Commit ADR-000** — the decision to use ADRs. Rationale only; the template lives in its own file (`decisions/_template.md`). The template should include Failure Criteria and Definition of Done sections from the start — retrofitting them later requires grandfathering decisions.
    60|
    61|6. **Commit structural ADRs** (repo structure, hosting, tooling map, AI participant architecture) in dependency order. Co-dependent ADRs should be committed as a batch. Forward references between co-dependent ADRs are unavoidable during bootstrapping; batch acceptance resolves them.
    62|
    63|7. **Commit the lifecycle ADR** (ADR-005 equivalent) before accepting any ADR through the evaluator. This ADR should itself be the first to pass through the gate it defines. The evaluation record convention (where Eva's evaluations are stored) must be stated explicitly in this ADR — leaving it unspecified makes the Definition of Done mechanically unverifiable.
    64|
    65|### Phase 3 — The Evaluator
    66|
    67|8. **Initialise Eva** only after ADR-000, `principles.md`, and the glossary are committed. Eva needs stable ground truth before evaluation is useful. Eva's value increases with richer ground truth — the more committed artefacts she can reason against, the more precise her findings.
    68|
    69|9. **Run the lifecycle ADR through Eva first.** Then submit the structural ADR batch. The two-round evaluation cycle (first pass → targeted fixes → second pass) is the standard pattern for revision-heavy batches.
    70|
    71|### Phase 4 — Derived Artefacts
    72|
    73|10. **Commit `architecture.json`** (or equivalent registry) after structural ADRs are accepted. The registry is derived from decisions, not prior to them. Include a `governance_status` field with at least three states: `governed`, `pre_governance`, `pending`.
    74|
    75|11. **Commit `roadmap.md`** after the first implementation phase is scoped. The roadmap's only genuine function is strategic orientation — task tracking, completion records, and pending-role tables all have better homes elsewhere.
    76|
    77|---
    78|
    79|## The Participant Architecture
    80|
    81|The framework operates with four cognitive modes. These modes are more transferable than the specific participant names — a simpler project might need fewer named participants, but the modes themselves are the minimum viable governance architecture.
    82|
    83|**Generate** (Gov / S4) — Produces architectural intent. Asks: *what must be true?* Owns ADRs, specs, and the governance reasoning record. The generative layer's identity is "the reasoning record that shapes future reconstructions" — not "the map." The distinction matters: a layer that understands itself as "the map" gradually absorbs territory concerns; a layer that understands itself as "the reasoning record" has a clearer rejection criterion for what doesn't belong.
    84|
    85|**Evaluate** (Eva / S3*) — Adversarial compliance verification. Asks: *is this actually true? Is this what we said we would do?* Eva is not a code reviewer or a QA layer. Eva is a governance auditor. Her question at every boundary is whether the output honours the commitments made before implementation began. Eva's most productive findings cluster at the interfaces between artefact types, not within them — a structural property of adversarial evaluation that the generative layer cannot replicate.
    86|
    87|**Coordinate** (Nav / S2) — Translates intent into executable delivery contracts. Asks: *how do we make this true?* Nav should be a pure function: repos in, drafts out. No persistent state, no maintained artefacts, no governance overhead beyond the initial declaration ADR. The moment a coordination participant starts maintaining its own artefacts, it acquires governance weight that defeats its purpose.
    88|
    89|**Observe** (Adi / outside the VSM stack) — Surfaces patterns, fragilities, and transferable insights. Asks: *what are we learning about how we make things true?* Adi does not participate in governance decisions. Adi's channel is observation files; they accumulate slowly and accurately.
    90|
    91|### The generator/evaluator separation is load-bearing
    92|
    93|The temptation to use a single AI context for both drafting and evaluating is real, especially early when the project is small. Resist it. Eva's value comes from arriving cold to every proposal. A generator asked to critique its own output will tend toward self-confirmation. The separation is enforced by project boundaries (separate Claude Projects, separate system prompts, separate context), not by instruction.
    94|
    95|### Participant system prompts must carry constitutional reasoning
    96|
    97|Each participant's system prompt should explain not just what the role does, but why it is structurally separate and what breaks if roles collapse. A prompt that says "you are the evaluator" is weaker than one that says "you are the evaluator because a generator asked to critique its own output tends toward self-confirmation, and the governance gate's value depends on you arriving cold to every proposal."
    98|
    99|### Role discomfort is the leading indicator of a missing participant
   100|
   101|If reasoning consistently feels uncomfortable in a role — if Gov feels "out of character" doing translation work, or if the Gardener is performing coordination that no participant owns — the framework is telling you a new role exists. The path is: feel the misfit → name the concern → locate it in the VSM → declare the participant. This is how Nav was discovered.
   102|
   103|---
   104|
   105|## The Two-Repo Architecture
   106|
   107|The framework separates a governance repository (Gov, the map) from an implementation repository (Imp, the territory). This separation is not organisational convenience — it is the mechanism that keeps the reasoning record stable as the implementation evolves.
   108|
   109|**The one-way reference direction is the structural invariant.** Imp artefacts accumulate references to Gov; Gov never accumulates references to Imp. This holds at every phase of the lifecycle without exception. It is what keeps the map stable as the territory grows.
   110|
   111|**Gov describes roles; Imp names instances.** Gov documents describe what a node must be, what a service must guarantee, what constraints apply. The Imp manifest records what currently satisfies those roles — specific hardware, current IPs, OS versions. These are different kinds of facts with different rates of change. Mixing them in Gov documents creates a maintenance burden that scales with infrastructure growth.
   112|
   113|**Reproducibility and evolvability are different properties.** Imp + hardware + the bootstrap spark = reproducibility (the system can be rebuilt). Gov = evolvability (the next rebuild can be better than the last). Both are necessary; only the first set is necessary to reconstruct the running system. Any project that conflates "what do you need to run this" with "what do you need to improve this" will produce governance artefacts that over-specify operational dependencies.
   114|
   115|---
   116|
   117|## The Delivery Lifecycle
   118|
   119|The Gov→Imp handoff follows a three-artefact pattern, evaluated at three gates:
   120|
   121|**Gov spec** (enduring requirements) → **Eva gate 1** → **Imp contract** (delivery-scoped commitment: spec + plan + prompt) → **Eva gate 2** → **Autonomous execution on branch** → **Eva gate 3** → **Gardener merge**
   122|
   123|### Key principles
   124|
   125|**Define the interface before the first delivery crosses it.** The delivery lifecycle protocol must be committed (as an ADR) before the first component spec is written. If the first delivery invents its own protocol ad hoc, that protocol becomes implicit convention — never evaluated, difficult to supersede.
   126|
   127|**Specs outlive deliveries.** A Gov spec for a component might produce an initial delivery, then another when requirements change, then another when a new site comes online. The spec is the enduring anchor; the contract is the scoped commitment to satisfy it at a point in time.
   128|
   129|**The adoption-ADR-plus-spec pattern is a required two-artefact convention.** The ADR records the choice (why this tool over alternatives — a reasoning act). The spec records the contract (what the tool must guarantee — a specification act). Collapsing them into a single artefact embeds alternatives-reasoning into an operational contract, which is a category error.
   130|
   131|**The branch is the governance boundary for autonomous agents.** Autonomous execution (RWL or any agentic loop) commits freely on a branch. The governance gate fires at merge. The Gardener merges or doesn't. This is how you get execution velocity and governance integrity without forcing them into conflict.
   132|
   133|### The three-layer separation
   134|
   135|Within artefacts, separate what was decided (ADRs — immutable, slow) from how it's practised (conventions — revisable, medium) from what craft knowledge accumulates (guidelines — freely evolving, fast). Coupling fast-changing conventions to slow-changing governance decisions is the most common source of unnecessary supersession chains.
   136|
   137|---
   138|
   139|## Eva: Patterns and Anti-Patterns
   140|
   141|### What works
   142|
   143|**Pre-empting Eva's likely findings before submission.** Reading a draft adversarially for split records, prescriptive framing, untestable DoD items, and implicit assumptions before submitting reduces round-trips. This is not gaming the evaluation — it is exactly what the evaluation is designed to encourage. Eva's value is in catching what the drafter missed, not what the drafter already knew.
   144|
   145|**Submitting co-dependent ADRs as a batch.** Isolated submission of co-dependent artefacts generates blocking findings (forward references) that dissolve immediately under batch evaluation.
   146|
   147|**The two-round evaluation cycle as standard.** First pass → targeted fixes → second pass. Multiple blocking findings on first pass is the gate working correctly, not a signal that the artefacts weren't ready.
   148|
   149|**Eva's evaluation quality scales with artefact quality.** As the Gardener internalises Eva's patterns, pre-submission quality rises, findings become more advisory than blocking, and cycle velocity increases.
   150|
   151|### What to watch
   152|
   153|**Eva-predictability risk.** The feedback loop between Gardener learning and Eva patterns can become a game of "pass Eva" rather than genuine quality improvement. The check is the delivery itself — does the governed artefact produce a working system?
   154|
   155|**Cross-spec consistency is an open problem.** Eva evaluates proposals against a Gardener-curated context set. If the Gardener doesn't include related specs, Eva can't catch contradictions between them. The risk scales with spec count.
   156|
   157|**Eva finds interfaces, not internals.** Eva's most productive findings consistently cluster at the boundaries between artefact types — where a spec meets the registry, where a version field meets the compliance gate. Expect this and value it.
   158|
   159|### Eva placement
   160|
   161|Eva occupies S3* in the VSM — Beer's independent audit channel, not S3 (operational control). The distinction is non-obvious but structurally important: S3 is health checks and local logic; S3* bypasses the normal hierarchy to provide unbiased verification. Eva's constitutional adversarialism is an S3* property.
   162|
   163|---
   164|
   165|## Brownfield Adoption
   166|
   167|Every real deployment arrives onto terrain that already has tools, networks, and services in place. The framework handles greenfield elegantly (spec first, ADR first). Brownfield requires a retroactive registration path.
   168|
   169|**The `pre_governance` status** makes ungoverned components a declared condition rather than a silent gap. The registry's `governance_status` field (`governed`, `pre_governance`, `pending`) is the entry point. A retroactive ADR is the path from `pre_governance` to `governed`.
   170|
   171|**Retroactive ADRs are structurally distinct from forward-looking ADRs.** The options-considered section requires particular care — for a tool already in production, the alternatives were often not evaluated at all, and the ADR must be honest about that rather than fabricating a selection rationale. An "adoption context" section may be more appropriate than "options considered."
   172|
   173|**The framework exerts a gravitational pull toward debt resolution.** When a sequencing principle (such as "govern the dependency first") requires every artefact to reference governing artefacts or declare debt explicitly, deferral becomes progressively more expensive — each new artefact that touches an ungoverned concept carries its own debt declaration. Resolution is bounded (one ADR closes the gap). This cost inversion is emergent, not designed, and is one of the framework's most powerful properties.
   174|
   175|---
   176|
   177|## Governance Patterns Worth Naming
   178|
   179|### Governance failures are silent; execution failures are loud
   180|
   181|This explains why execution tooling (CI/CD, orchestration, deployment automation) has broad adoption while governance-layer tooling remains rare. A missing test breaks the build. A missing ADR breaks nothing — until it breaks everything, months later, when nobody can reconstruct why a decision was made. The framework's primary value is making governance failures visible before they compound.
   182|
   183|### The split-record rule
   184|
   185|Any time a governance artefact declares another document as authoritative for a piece of information, that information must not also appear in the declaring artefact. The declaration is the pointer; duplicating the content creates a split record. This recurs whenever mapping tables, principles, or lifecycle definitions are referenced across artefacts. Supersede rather than forward-reference.
   186|
   187|### Analytical framework vs. design methodology
   188|
   189|When adopting a theoretical framework (VSM, Domain-Driven Design, etc.) as a lens for reasoning, commit a decision that names its scope and its limits. Analytical frameworks provide vocabulary and diagnostics; they do not generate prescriptive authority. The failure mode is decisions justified by "VSM requires" rather than by project-specific reasoning through the VSM lens.
   190|
   191|### The lifecycle walkthrough as audit instrument
   192|
   193|Before deploying this framework in a new context, walk a hypothetical feature through all lifecycle phases and check whether every phase produces a committable artefact in the right repo. Phases that produce nothing, or touch the wrong layer, are gaps before implementation begins.
   194|
   195|### Document identity crisis as a design smell
   196|
   197|A document trying to serve multiple concerns will grow unwieldy. The resolution is not better organisation — it is asking which functions have better homes elsewhere. The roadmap's identity crisis (task tracking + completion records + strategic orientation) resolved by routing each function to its natural home. What remained was thin and stable.
   198|
   199|### Practice-first, convention-second
   200|
   201|A framework observation that doesn't yet have three instances is a hypothesis, not a pattern. Filing it is appropriate. Building convention around it is premature. Let friction accumulate rather than pre-building structure for friction you haven't felt yet.
   202|
   203|### The complexity test
   204|
   205|Both the governance layer and the execution layer, when understood correctly, resolve to something embarrassingly simple. ADRs in Git, a Claude Project connected via GitHub, a human with commit authority. A bash while-loop and three markdown files. If your implementation of this framework feels complex, you are probably solving a problem the framework was designed to make unnecessary. The complexity of a solution is inversely correlated with how correctly the problem was framed.
   206|
   207|---
   208|
   209|## The Bootstrap Checklist
   210|
   211|### Before writing ADR-000
   212|
   213|- [ ] Canonical names for all repositories committed to README
   214|- [ ] Glossary stub defining all role terms including dormant participants
   215|- [ ] `principles.md` stub with core invariants
   216|- [ ] `decisions/_template.md` — standalone template with Failure Criteria and Definition of Done
   217|
   218|### On first commit
   219|
   220|- [ ] `README.md` with correct repo name, directory structure, and naming conventions
   221|- [ ] `decisions/_template.md` — the template is a file, not a code block inside ADR-000
   222|- [ ] `decisions/ADR-000-record-decisions.md` — rationale only, no embedded template
   223|- [ ] `principles.md` — standing constraints including Gardener role definition
   224|- [ ] `glossary.md` — canonical terms, abbreviations, all participant roles (active and dormant)
   225|
   226|### Before initialising Eva
   227|
   228|- [ ] At least ADR-000 and `principles.md` are committed
   229|- [ ] Eva's evaluation record convention is stated in the lifecycle ADR
   230|- [ ] Eva's system prompt carries constitutional reasoning, not just instructions
   231|
   232|### Before the first delivery
   233|
   234|- [ ] Delivery lifecycle protocol committed as an ADR (the Gov→Imp interface)
   235|- [ ] Three-artefact convention defined (spec, plan, prompt) with naming that makes boundary provenance self-evident
   236|- [ ] Evaluation gates defined: artefact evaluation, contract evaluation, branch evaluation
   237|- [ ] The registry supports `governance_status` for brownfield components
   238|
   239|### Before publication
   240|
   241|- [ ] At least two complete deliveries demonstrating the full lifecycle
   242|- [ ] Navigation participant operational (or the coordination function exercised)
   243|- [ ] Compliance gate active
   244|- [ ] Bootstrap lessons document reflects actual practice, not aspirational practice
   245|
   246|---
   247|
   248|## What Can Be Deferred
   249|
   250|These were referenced in early artefacts but should not block the bootstrap:
   251|
   252|- `architecture.json` — commit after structural ADRs are accepted
   253|- `roadmap.md` — commit after the first implementation phase is scoped
   254|- `specs/` directory — create when the first component spec is needed
   255|- `exploring/` directory — create when the first exploration note is worth preserving
   256|- Automated compliance (SemaphoreUI + Claude API or equivalent) — defer until Imp is operational
   257|- The immune system / drift detection — defer until the compliance gate has exercised at least once
   258|- GitHub Projects or equivalent task surface — defer until the wup-based workflow feels insufficient
   259|- The reverse arrow (Imp→Gov signal path) — acknowledge but don't formalise until you have three concrete instances
   260|
   261|---
   262|
   263|## The Maturity Signals
   264|
   265|How to know the framework is working:
   266|
   267|**Past mistakes prevent future ones, not just record them.** The framework is compounding forward when a design heuristic learned from an early error ("will this convention need to evolve faster than the ADR?") is applied proactively to prevent the same error in a new context.
   268|
   269|**The framework tells you what it needs.** Role discomfort signals a missing participant. Repeated debt declarations signal a governance gap that's cheaper to resolve than to keep carrying. Evaluation findings cluster at a boundary that hasn't been committed yet. The framework's feedback is structural, not conversational.
   270|
   271|**Sessions that honour the process when it's inconvenient produce better artefacts.** The framework's value is most visible when it's most inconvenient. A framework that only governs easy decisions isn't governing — it's decorating. The quality of a governance framework is measured by the decisions it prevents you from deferring, not by the decisions it automates.
   272|
   273|**Tension between participants is productive, not pathological.** When the generative layer proposes and the evaluator blocks, when the observer recommends and the generator pushes back, when the Gardener sides with restraint over action — these are the framework's multi-participant model working as designed. A single participant handling all perspectives would collapse to one side.
   274|
   275|**Convergence with independently derived systems is a validation signal.** If your governance framework fits naturally with an execution pattern you had never seen, you were probably reasoning from the right foundations. Convergence of independently derived primitives is stronger evidence than any internal consistency check.

     2|
     3|> A recursively self-modifying, memory-preserving, adversarially-evaluated institution for governing AI-participant infrastructure that produces functioning systems.
     4|
     5|Stirps is a governance framework for long-lived technical projects that use AI as participants. The system remembers, not the AI. Every AI participant is stateless and replaceable; reasoning continuity lives in the governance record.
     6|
     7|## Core Principles
     8|
     9|- Governance precedes the thing it governs. Map before territory.
    10|- The institution is at the centre. AI participants are at the periphery.
    11|- One human (the Gardener) holds sole commit authority. This is load-bearing, not a bottleneck.
    12|- Decisions are recorded as immutable, append-only Architecture Decision Records.
    13|- Four cognitive modes — Generate, Evaluate, Coordinate, Observe — must be structurally separated.
    14|- The evaluator arrives cold to every proposal. Self-confirmation is the default failure mode of any reasoning system.
    15|- The session model is human-paced. AI participants do not run unsupervised.
    16|
    17|## Architecture
    18|
    19|- Two repositories: Gov (the map — decisions, specs, principles) and Imp (the territory — contracts, playbooks, configuration).
    20|- Reference direction is asymmetric: territory points to map; map never contains territory.
    21|- Delivery lifecycle: Gov spec → Evaluation gate → Contract → Evaluation gate → Branch execution → Evaluation gate → Gardener merge.
    22|- Three-state component model: governed, pre_governance, pending.
    23|
    24|## Primitives
    25|
    26|- Governance record: version-controlled repository (Git + Markdown)
    27|- Decision format: Architecture Decision Records
    28|- Machine-readable registry: structured data with schema validation (JSON)
    29|- Execution loop: stateless agent loop with external completion check
    30|- AI reasoning participants: isolated model sessions, one per cognitive mode
    31|

    33|
    34|- Website: https://stirps.ai
    35|- Fork the framework: https://github.com/stirps-ai/stirps-gov

    16|across contributors, tools, and time. AI participants are stateless — they
    17|reconstruct context from committed artefacts, not from conversational memory.
    18|Without a structured decision record, each session reasons from an incomplete

    42|Numbers are never reused. Gaps in numbering are permitted if a proposed ADR is

    48||---|---|
    49|| Proposed | Drafted, awaiting adversarial evaluation |
