    83|- Every significant architectural choice produces a committed, immutable record.
    84|- AI participants can reconstruct decision context from the ADR chain without
    85|  access to prior conversations.
    86|- The evaluation gate introduces a mandatory adversarial review step, which
    87|  adds latency to decision acceptance but catches structural defects before
    88|  they compound.
    89|- The template is decoupled from this ADR — template evolution does not require
    90|  superseding the foundational governance decision.
    91|
    92|---
    93|
    94|## Failure Criteria
    95|
    96|- ADRs are routinely edited after acceptance rather than superseded.
    97|- The evaluation gate is bypassed for convenience without recording the
    98|  exception.
    99|- Decision context is routinely reconstructed from conversations rather
   100|  than from committed ADRs, indicating the records are not serving their
   101|  purpose.
   102|
   103|---
   104|
   105|## Definition of Done
   106|
   107|- [x] This ADR is committed to the governance repository (grandfathered)
   108|- [x] `decisions/_template.md` exists as a standalone file
   109|- [x] `decisions/evals/` directory exists for evaluation records
   110|- [x] `glossary.md` defines "ADR", "Gardener", and "Evaluate" terms
   111|
   112|**Note:** This repository ships `glossary-template.md`. References to
   113|`glossary.md` in DoD items are satisfied after the forker copies the
   114|template to `glossary.md` per BOOTSTRAP.md Step 2.
   115|", "total_lines": 114, "file_size": 4372, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-001: Two-Repository Structure
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|The project produces two fundamentally different kinds of artefacts:
    16|governance artefacts (decisions, principles, specifications) and
    17|implementation artefacts (configurations, playbooks, contracts). These have
    18|different lifecycles, different rates of change, and different audiences.
    19|
    20|Mixing them in a single repository creates coupling between the reasoning
    21|record and the operational record. Changes to implementation details
    22|appear in the same commit history as architectural decisions, making it
    23|difficult for AI participants to reconstruct governance context without
    24|parsing operational noise.
    25|
    26|---
    27|
    28|## Decision
    29|
    30|The project maintains two repositories:
    31|
    32|**Governance repository (the map).** Contains ADRs, principles, glossary,
    33|component specifications, the architecture registry, and evaluation records.
    34|Describes what must be true. Changes slowly. The Gardener is the sole
    35|committer.
    36|
    37|**Implementation repository (the territory).** Contains playbooks,
    38|configurations, delivery contracts, operational manifests, and all artefacts
    39|required to build and run the system. Satisfies what the governance
    40|repository requires. Changes frequently.
    41|
    42|**Reference direction.** Implementation artefacts reference governance
    43|artefacts (e.g. a delivery contract references a Gov spec). Governance
    44|artefacts never reference implementation artefacts by path or name. The
    45|governance repository describes roles and constraints; the implementation
    46|repository names the instances that satisfy them.
    47|
    48|This separation is structural, not organisational. The two repositories
    49|serve different functions at different layers of time.
    50|
    51|---
    52|
    53|## Options Considered
    54|
    55|### Single repository with directory separation
    56|
    57|Governance and implementation in one repo, separated by directories. Rejected:
    58|Git history, branch policies, and access patterns differ between the two
    59|concerns. A single repo forces shared branching strategy and makes it
    60|impossible to grant AI execution agents write access to implementation
    61|without exposing governance artefacts.
    62|
    63|### Two repositories as described above
    64|
    65|Chosen for: clean separation of concerns, independent lifecycles, and the
    66|structural enforcement of one-way reference direction.
    67|
    68|---
    69|
    70|## Consequences
    71|
    72|- AI participants reasoning about governance load only the governance
    73|  repository — no implementation noise in context.
    74|- The implementation repository can grant write access to autonomous
    75|  execution agents (on branches) without risking governance artefacts.
    76|- The one-way reference direction is now a verifiable invariant: any
    77|  governance artefact that references an implementation path is a defect.
    78|- Both repositories are necessary to the project. Only the implementation
    79|  repository, the bootstrap secret, and the infrastructure are necessary
    80|  to reconstruct the running system.
    81|
    82|---
    83|
    84|## Failure Criteria
    85|
    86|- Governance artefacts accumulate implementation-specific paths, IPs, or
    87|  version numbers.
    88|- Implementation artefacts are committed to the governance repository
    89|  "for convenience."
    90|- The governance repository's commit frequency approaches the implementation
    91|  repository's, suggesting operational content is leaking into governance.
    92|
    93|---
    94|
    95|## Definition of Done
    96|
    97|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
    98|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
    99|- [ ] `glossary.md` updated with "Map" and "Territory" definitions
   100|- [ ] `principles.md` "One-way reference" principle confirmed present
   101|", "total_lines": 100, "file_size": 3762, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-002: Repository Hosting Platform
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|Both repositories require a hosting platform that supports Git version control,
    16|is accessible to all participants (human and AI), and provides integration
    17|points for evaluation and delivery workflows. The governance repository is the
    18|project's institutional memory — its hosting must be reliable, widely
    19|accessible, and not dependent on infrastructure the project itself manages.
    20|
    21|---
    22|
    23|## Decision
    24|
    25|Both repositories are hosted on a chosen Git platform.
    26|
    27|<!-- Replace with your platform: GitHub, GitLab, Gitea, etc. -->
    28|
    29|**Accessibility.** The platform must be accessible from all environments where
    30|participants operate — browser-based interfaces, API access for AI tooling,
    31|and command-line Git.
    32|
    33|**External dependency.** The hosting platform is an external point of failure
    34|acknowledged by this ADR. The governance framework depends on a service the
    35|project does not control. This is acceptable at bootstrap; self-hosted Git
    36|may become a deliberate infrastructure goal as the project matures.
    37|
    38|**Migration trigger.** If the platform becomes inaccessible, unreliable, or
    39|introduces constraints that conflict with the governance model, migration to
    40|an alternative platform is triggered. The ADR chain, principles, and all
    41|governance artefacts are plain files in Git — platform migration is a
    42|hosting change, not a governance change.
    43|
    44|---
    45|
    46|## Options Considered
    47|
    48|### Self-hosted Git from the start
    49|
    50|Host repositories on project-managed infrastructure. Rejected at bootstrap:
    51|this creates a circular dependency — the governance framework would depend on
    52|infrastructure that the governance framework is used to build. Violates the
    53|"bootstrap layer is dependency-free" principle.
    54|
    55|### External hosted platform
    56|
    57|Chosen for: availability before any infrastructure exists, accessibility from
    58|all environments, and zero operational burden at bootstrap.
    59|
    60|---
    61|
    62|## Consequences
    63|
    64|- The governance layer is operational before any project infrastructure exists.
    65|- All artefacts are plain Git — no platform lock-in beyond hosting convenience.
    66|- AI participants with platform integrations can read repositories directly.
    67|- The external hosting dependency is declared, not hidden.
    68|
    69|---
    70|
    71|## Failure Criteria
    72|
    73|- The platform introduces access restrictions that prevent AI participants
    74|  from reading the governance repository.
    75|- Platform-specific features (issues, wikis, CI) become load-bearing
    76|  governance artefacts that cannot be migrated with Git alone.
    77|- Self-hosted Git becomes available but migration is deferred indefinitely,
    78|  leaving the external dependency unresolved.
    79|
    80|---
    81|
    82|## Definition of Done
    83|
    84|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
    85|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
    86|- [ ] Both repositories created on the chosen platform
    87|- [ ] README updated with repository URLs
    88|", "total_lines": 87, "file_size": 3130, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-003: Participant Architecture
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|The project requires multiple modes of reasoning — generative, evaluative,
    16|coordinative, and observational — that must be structurally separated to
    17|function correctly. A single AI context handling all modes tends toward
    18|self-confirmation (when generating and evaluating) and scope creep (when
    19|coordinating and deciding).
    20|
    21|Declaring participants incrementally as they are needed risks a supersession
    22|chain — each new role requires amending the architecture ADR, producing
    23|partial supersessions that fragment the participant record. Declaring all
    24|participants simultaneously, with dormancy states for roles not yet active,
    25|eliminates this risk at the cost of one glossary entry per dormant role.
    26|
    27|---
    28|
    29|## Decision
    30|
    31|The project operates with four AI participant roles and one human role,
    32|declared here in full. All participant terms are defined in `glossary.md`.
    33|
    34|| Role | Cognitive Mode | Status at Bootstrap | Activation Trigger |
    35||---|---|---|---|
    36|| Gardener | Human authority (S5) | Active | Always present |
    37|| Generate | Generative (S4) | Active | Reasoning begins at ADR-000 |
    38|| Evaluate | Adversarial audit (S3*) | Active | Evaluation gate defined in ADR-000 |
    39|| Coordinate | Coordination (S2) | Dormant | First delivery lifecycle completion |
    40|| Observe | Meta-observation | Dormant | Framework structurally complete; Gardener discretion |
    41|
    42|**Note:** VSM positions (S5, S4, S3*, S2) in this table are a forward
    43|reference to ADR-004, which adopts the analytical framework. Both ADRs
    44|are co-dependent within the bootstrap batch.
    45|
    46|**The Gardener** holds sole commit authority. All AI participants are advisory.
    47|The Gardener approves, commits, merges, or rejects at every boundary.
    48|
    49|**The Generate participant** reasons forward from committed decisions. Proposes
    50|ADRs and specifications. Owns the architectural reasoning record. Its identity
    51|is "the reasoning record that shapes future reconstructions" — not "the map."
    52|The distinction prevents the generative layer from absorbing implementation
    53|concerns.
    54|
    55|**The Evaluate participant** evaluates adversarially against the committed
    56|record. Never proposes, only judges. Must never share a reasoning context with
    57|the Generate participant — the separation is enforced by separate environments
    58|(separate projects, separate system prompts, separate conversations), not by
    59|instruction.
    60|
    61|**The Coordinate participant** translates committed architectural intent into
    62|executable delivery contracts. Maintains no artefacts. A pure function:
    63|repositories in, drafts out. The moment a coordination participant starts
    64|maintaining its own artefacts, it acquires governance weight that defeats its
    65|purpose. Begins dormant; activated when the delivery lifecycle needs
    66|coordination.
    67|
    68|**The Observe participant** surfaces patterns, fragilities, and transferable
    69|insights across sessions. Excluded from governance decisions. Its channel is
    70|observation files that accumulate slowly and accurately. Begins dormant;
    71|activated at the Gardener's discretion when there is enough framework history
    72|to observe.
    73|
    74|**Dormancy.** A dormant participant is declared in this ADR and defined in
    75|the glossary, but has no active environment (no Claude Project, no system
    76|prompt in use). Activation is an operational event — the Gardener sets up
    77|the environment — not a governance event requiring a new ADR.
    78|
    79|**System prompts.** Each participant's system prompt must carry constitutional
    80|reasoning: not just what the role does, but why it is structurally separate
    81|and what breaks if roles collapse. System prompts are Gardener-maintained
    82|craft artefacts, not governance artefacts — they iterate freely.
    83|
    84|---
    85|
    86|## Options Considered
    87|
    88|### Incremental participant declaration
    89|
    90|Declare participants as they are needed. Rejected: incremental declaration
    91|tends to require partial supersessions as new roles emerge — each new
    92|participant amends the architecture ADR. The cost of declaring a dormant
    93|participant is one glossary entry and one row in this ADR. The cost of
    94|retroactive declaration is a supersession chain.
    95|
    96|### Full declaration with dormancy states
    97|
    98|Chosen for: architectural completeness from day one, elimination of
    99|supersession chains, and explicit activation triggers that make dormancy
   100|a designed state rather than an omission.
   101|
   102|---
   103|
   104|## Consequences
   105|
   106|- The participant architecture is complete at bootstrap. No structural ADR
   107|  amendments are needed when dormant roles activate.
   108|- Each role has a defined cognitive mode and a clear rejection criterion
   109|  for reasoning that belongs elsewhere.
   110|- The generator/evaluator separation is enforced by environment boundaries,
   111|  not by instruction — this is a structural commitment, not a convention.
   112|- If a dormant role's design changes significantly before activation, this
   113|  ADR may need amendment. This is a small risk traded against the v1 cost
   114|  of incremental declaration.
   115|
   116|---
   117|
   118|## Failure Criteria
   119|
   120|- The Generate and Evaluate participants share a reasoning context (same
   121|  project, same conversation, same system prompt).
   122|- The Coordinate participant begins maintaining persistent artefacts.
   123|- A new participant role emerges that cannot be mapped to one of the four
   124|  cognitive modes — suggesting the mode model is incomplete.
   125|- Role discomfort is ignored rather than treated as a signal of a missing
   126|  or misconfigured participant.
   127|
   128|---
   129|
   130|## Definition of Done
   131|
   132|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
   133|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
   134|- [ ] `glossary.md` updated with all participant definitions
   135|- [ ] All participant terms used in prior ADRs are consistent with this declaration
   136|", "total_lines": 135, "file_size": 5929, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-004: Analytical Framework Adoption
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|The participant architecture (ADR-003) defines four cognitive modes and a
    16|human authority. These roles need a diagnostic vocabulary — a way to reason
    17|about their relationships, boundaries, and failure modes. An analytical
    18|framework provides this vocabulary without generating prescriptive authority.
    19|
    20|The framework chosen must be adopted as a lens for reasoning, not as a
    21|design methodology. The failure mode is decisions justified by "the framework
    22|requires" rather than by project-specific reasoning through the framework's
    23|lens.
    24|
    25|<!-- Replace the framework below with your project's choice — VSM, DDD,
    26|     TOGAF, or any other lens. The ADR structure is transferable; the
    27|     specific framework is not. -->
    28|
    29|---
    30|
    31|## Decision
    32|
    33|The Viable System Model (VSM) is adopted as the analytical framework for
    34|reasoning about participant relationships and system structure.
    35|
    36|**Scope.** VSM provides vocabulary and diagnostics. It does not generate
    37|prescriptive authority. ADRs are justified by project-specific reasoning,
    38|not by VSM prescription.
    39|
    40|**Canonical record.** The glossary's Theoretical Anchors section is the
    41|single authoritative VSM mapping. No mapping table appears in this ADR
    42|or any other artefact — the glossary is the single source of truth. This
    43|eliminates split-record risk.
    44|
    45|**Re-expression corollary.** When drift is observed between the VSM mapping
    46|and the project's actual structure, the correct response is to update the
    47|mapping to reflect reality — not to remediate the running system to match
    48|the theoretical model. The map serves the territory, not the reverse.
    49|
    50|---
    51|
    52|## Options Considered
    53|
    54|### No analytical framework
    55|
    56|Reason about participant relationships using project-specific vocabulary only.
    57|Viable for simple projects. Rejected for this project because the four-mode
    58|architecture benefits from a diagnostic vocabulary that helps identify
    59|structural gaps (e.g. "this concern sits at S2, which is unoccupied").
    60|
    61|### VSM as analytical lens
    62|
    63|Chosen for: rich vocabulary for multi-participant systems, explicit attention
    64|to audit channels (S3*), and alignment with the project's self-governing
    65|architecture.
    66|
    67|---
    68|
    69|## Consequences
    70|
    71|- Participants can be located in a theoretical structure, making structural
    72|  gaps visible.
    73|- The Evaluate participant's placement at S3* (independent audit) rather than
    74|  S3 (operational control) is an explicit governance decision with clear
    75|  rationale.
    76|- The glossary's Theoretical Anchors section becomes the canonical mapping —
    77|  all other references point there.
    78|- VSM vocabulary enters the governance record but carries no prescriptive
    79|  weight — it is always filtered through project-specific reasoning.
    80|
    81|---
    82|
    83|## Failure Criteria
    84|
    85|- Decisions are justified by "VSM requires" rather than by project-specific
    86|  reasoning.
    87|- The VSM mapping in the glossary diverges from the project's actual structure
    88|  without anyone noticing.
    89|- The framework's vocabulary becomes jargon that obscures reasoning rather
    90|  than clarifying it.
    91|
    92|---
    93|
    94|## Definition of Done
    95|
    96|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
    97|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
    98|- [ ] `glossary.md` Theoretical Anchors section populated with the VSM mapping
    99|- [ ] No VSM mapping table exists outside the glossary
   100|", "total_lines": 99, "file_size": 3593, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-005: Concern-to-Tool Mapping
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|The project uses multiple tools, each serving a specific concern. Without a
    16|canonical record of which tool serves which function, concerns accumulate
    17|implicit tool assignments that are never evaluated and difficult to audit.
    18|
    19|Embedding a mapping table inside an ADR body forces a full supersession for
    20|every new row — a pattern that produces unnecessary supersession chains for
    21|what is essentially a growing lookup table.
    22|
    23|---
    24|
    25|## Decision
    26|
    27|Each concern in the project maps to a single designated tool. The mapping is
    28|maintained as a governed reference file.
    29|
    30|**Mapping model.** Each row records: concern → tool → governing ADR →
    31|responsible participant.
    32|
    33|**Reference file.** The mapping table lives in `mappings/concern-to-tool.md`,
    34|not inline in this ADR. This ADR governs the mapping *model* — what the table
    35|means, how it's structured, what consistency rules it must satisfy. Subsequent
    36|ADRs that adopt new tools or introduce new concerns add rows to the reference
    37|file and cite this ADR as the governing authority.
    38|
    39|**Consistency rules:**
    40|
    41|- Every tool in the mapping must be governed by an accepted ADR.
    42|- Every participant in the mapping must be declared in ADR-003.
    43|- No speculative rows. The mapping reflects current reality at the time of
    44|  each update.
    45|- One concern, one tool. If two tools serve the same concern, one must be
    46|  designated primary and the other noted as an exception with rationale.
    47|
    48|**Extensibility.** Adding a new tool to the mapping requires: (1) an adoption
    49|ADR for the tool, (2) a row added to the reference file citing both the
    50|adoption ADR and this ADR. No supersession of this ADR is needed.
    51|
    52|---
    53|
    54|## Options Considered
    55|
    56|### Mapping table inline in this ADR
    57|
    58|Rejected: every new row requires a superseding ADR, which
    59|is heavyweight for what is essentially a lookup table update.
    60|
    61|### Mapping model in ADR, table in reference file
    62|
    63|Chosen for: the model (which changes rarely) is governed by an immutable ADR;
    64|the table (which grows) is a Gardener-maintained file governed by that ADR.
    65|
    66|---
    67|
    68|## Consequences
    69|
    70|- New tool adoptions extend the mapping without superseding this ADR.
    71|- The reference file is auditable: every row must trace to an accepted ADR.
    72|- The mapping provides a single place to answer "what tool do we use for X?"
    73|- The consistency rules make the mapping mechanically verifiable.
    74|
    75|---
    76|
    77|## Failure Criteria
    78|
    79|- Tools are adopted and used without a corresponding row in the mapping.
    80|- The reference file grows rows that have no governing ADR.
    81|- This ADR requires supersession to accommodate a new tool — indicating the
    82|  extensibility mechanism has failed.
    83|
    84|---
    85|
    86|## Definition of Done
    87|
    88|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
    89|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
    90|- [ ] `mappings/concern-to-tool.md` created with initial rows for all
    91|      currently governed tools
    92|- [ ] Consistency rules are mechanically verifiable against the reference file
    93|", "total_lines": 92, "file_size": 3255, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-006: Infrastructure Manifest
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|The project manages physical or virtual infrastructure with concrete instance
    16|facts: node names, IP addresses, hardware specifications, OS versions, domain
    17|assignments. These facts change at operational speed and belong in a different
    18|artefact from architectural decisions, which change at governance speed.
    19|
    20|Governance artefacts describe structural identity — what a node must be, what
    21|a service must guarantee. The manifest records what currently satisfies those
    22|roles. Mixing them creates a maintenance burden that scales with infrastructure
    23|growth.
    24|
    25|---
    26|
    27|## Decision
    28|
    29|The infrastructure manifest is the authoritative record of instance facts.
    30|
    31|**Location.** The manifest lives in the implementation repository. Governance
    32|artefacts reference roles and constraints; the manifest names the instances
    33|that satisfy them.
    34|
    35|**Content.** The manifest records: node identifiers, network addresses,
    36|hardware specifications, OS versions, site assignments, and any other
    37|instance-specific facts that governance artefacts must not contain.
    38|
    39|**Boundary.** Governance documents describe what a node role requires.
    40|The manifest records which specific nodes currently fill those roles. This
    41|is the operational expression of the one-way reference principle: governance
    42|describes the structural identity; the manifest describes the concrete
    43|instances.
    44|
    45|---
    46|
    47|## Options Considered
    48|
    49|### Instance facts in governance documents
    50|
    51|Include IPs, hardware specs, and node names in Gov specs or ADRs. Rejected:
    52|every hardware change or IP reassignment would require governance artefact
    53|updates, coupling governance cadence to operational cadence.
    54|
    55|### Separate manifest in the implementation repository
    56|
    57|Chosen for: clean separation of concerns between structural identity
    58|(governance) and concrete instances (manifest), with different rates of
    59|change respected.
    60|
    61|---
    62|
    63|## Consequences
    64|
    65|- Governance artefacts remain stable as infrastructure scales or changes.
    66|- The manifest is the single place to look up "what is currently deployed."
    67|- AI participants reasoning about architecture load governance artefacts
    68|  without operational noise; those reasoning about deployment load the
    69|  manifest.
    70|- The Coordinate participant uses both — the spec (from governance) and the
    71|  manifest (from implementation) — to draft delivery contracts.
    72|
    73|---
    74|
    75|## Failure Criteria
    76|
    77|- Governance artefacts accumulate IP addresses, node names, or OS versions.
    78|- The manifest falls out of sync with deployed reality and no process
    79|  corrects the drift.
    80|- Multiple artefacts claim to be the authoritative source of instance facts.
    81|
    82|---
    83|
    84|## Definition of Done
    85|
    86|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
    87|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
    88|- [ ] Manifest location in the implementation repository is stated
    89|- [ ] No instance facts appear in any governance artefact
    90|", "total_lines": 89, "file_size": 3167, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-007: Implementation Delivery Lifecycle
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|The governance repository produces specifications. The implementation
    16|repository produces running systems. The interface between them — how
    17|architectural intent becomes executable work — must be committed as a
    18|governance artefact before the first delivery crosses it.
    19|
    20|Without a committed lifecycle protocol, the first delivery invents its own
    21|protocol ad hoc. That protocol becomes implicit convention — never evaluated,
    22|difficult to supersede because it was never committed.
    23|
    24|---
    25|
    26|## Decision
    27|
    28|The delivery lifecycle follows five phases, evaluated at three gates:
    29|
    30|```
    31|Scope → Spec → Contract → Execute → Accept
    32|```
    33|
    34|**Phase 1 — Scope.** The Gardener identifies a delivery need and selects a
    35|component.
    36|
    37|**Phase 2 — Spec.** The Generate participant produces a Gov spec: enduring
    38|requirements for the component. The spec describes what the component must
    39|guarantee, not how to configure it. Evaluated by the Evaluate participant
    40|(Gate 1).
    41|
    42|**Phase 3 — Contract.** The Coordinate participant (or the Gardener, if
    43|coordination is dormant) translates the Gov spec into a delivery-scoped
    44|commitment: a specification scoped to this delivery, a task plan, and an
    45|execution prompt. Evaluated by the Evaluate participant (Gate 2).
    46|
    47|**Phase 4 — Execute.** Autonomous execution runs on a branch. The execution
    48|agent commits freely on the branch. The governance boundary is the branch —
    49|the agent has full velocity within it.
    50|
    51|**Phase 5 — Accept.** The Evaluate participant reviews the branch output
    52|against the Gov spec and contract (Gate 3). The Gardener merges or rejects.
    53|
    54|**Specs outlive deliveries.** A Gov spec may produce multiple deliveries as
    55|requirements evolve or new sites come online. The spec is the enduring anchor;
    56|the contract is the scoped commitment to satisfy it at a point in time.
    57|
    58|**The adoption-ADR-plus-spec pattern.** When a new tool is adopted, two
    59|artefacts are required: an ADR (why this tool — a reasoning act) and a spec
    60|(what it must guarantee — a specification act). Collapsing them into a single
    61|artefact embeds alternatives-reasoning into an operational contract, which is
    62|a category error.
    63|
    64|**Three-layer separation within artefacts.** Separate what was decided (ADRs —
    65|immutable, slow) from how it's practised (conventions — revisable, medium)
    66|from what craft knowledge accumulates (guidelines — freely evolving, fast).
    67|Coupling fast-changing conventions to slow-changing governance decisions is the
    68|most common source of unnecessary supersession chains.
    69|
    70|---
    71|
    72|## Options Considered
    73|
    74|### Ad hoc delivery protocol per component
    75|
    76|Let each delivery define its own handoff. Rejected: produces inconsistent
    77|interfaces that are never evaluated and cannot be audited.
    78|
    79|### Committed five-phase lifecycle with three evaluation gates
    80|
    81|Chosen for: consistent interface, mandatory adversarial review at each
    82|boundary, and clear separation between enduring specs and scoped contracts.
    83|
    84|---
    85|
    86|## Consequences
    87|
    88|- Every delivery follows the same protocol, making the process auditable.
    89|- The branch is the governance boundary for autonomous agents — execution
    90|  velocity and governance integrity coexist without conflict.
    91|- The Evaluate participant reviews at three distinct gates, each with a
    92|  different concern: spec quality, contract quality, implementation quality.
    93|- The Coordinate participant has a defined role in Phase 3, even if dormant
    94|  at bootstrap.
    95|- Conventions and guidelines iterate without ADR supersession.
    96|
    97|---
    98|
    99|## Failure Criteria
   100|
   101|- Deliveries bypass the evaluation gates "just this once."
   102|- The Gov spec and implementation contract are collapsed into a single
   103|  artefact, losing the distinction between enduring requirements and
   104|  scoped commitments.
   105|- The branch boundary is not respected — autonomous agents commit to main
   106|  directly.
   107|- Conventions are embedded in this ADR rather than maintained as separate
   108|  craft artefacts.
   109|
   110|---
   111|
   112|## Definition of Done
   113|
   114|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
   115|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
   116|- [ ] Evaluation record convention stated: `decisions/evals/eval-YYYYMMDD-HHMM-<subject>.md`
   117|- [ ] The three evaluation gates are named and their concerns distinguished
   118|- [ ] This ADR does not embed convention or guideline content that should
   119|      iterate independently
   120|", "total_lines": 119, "file_size": 4642, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-008: Automated Compliance Gate
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|The delivery lifecycle (ADR-007) defines three evaluation gates where the
    16|Evaluate participant reviews artefacts adversarially. These gates currently
    17|operate manually — the Gardener submits artefacts, the Evaluate participant
    18|reviews, findings are resolved, and the Gardener commits.
    19|
    20|As the project matures, some compliance checks become mechanical: does the
    21|registry entry match the spec? Does the contract reference the correct spec
    22|version? Are all required artefact sections present? These checks can be
    23|automated without replacing the Evaluate participant's adversarial judgment.
    24|
    25|---
    26|
    27|## Decision
    28|
    29|Automated compliance checking of implementation artefacts against governance
    30|constraints is adopted as an intent.
    31|
    32|**Position in the lifecycle.** The automated gate sits between contract
    33|acceptance and execution in the delivery lifecycle (between Gate 2 and
    34|Phase 4 of ADR-007). It verifies mechanical compliance before autonomous
    35|execution begins.
    36|
    37|**Scope.** The automated gate checks structural compliance: required fields
    38|present, references valid, naming conventions followed, registry consistency
    39|maintained. It does not replace the Evaluate participant's adversarial review
    40|of architectural quality, reasoning coherence, or governance integrity.
    41|
    42|**Implementation deferred.** This ADR records the architectural intent. The
    43|gate is activated when the execution tooling is operational and the first
    44|delivery lifecycle has been exercised manually. Premature automation of a
    45|process that hasn't been practised manually produces brittle checks against
    46|poorly understood requirements.
    47|
    48|**No mapping table update.** The compliance gate's position in the
    49|concern-to-tool mapping (ADR-005) is established when the gate is activated
    50|and a specific tool is chosen.
    51|
    52|---
    53|
    54|## Options Considered
    55|
    56|### Manual compliance only
    57|
    58|Rely entirely on the Evaluate participant for all compliance checking.
    59|Viable indefinitely for small projects. Rejected as a permanent state because
    60|mechanical checks consume evaluation context that would be better spent on
    61|architectural judgment.
    62|
    63|### Automated compliance gate as described above
    64|
    65|Chosen for: separation of mechanical checks from adversarial judgment,
    66|reduced evaluation round-trips for structural defects, and clear activation
    67|trigger (first manual lifecycle completion).
    68|
    69|---
    70|
    71|## Consequences
    72|
    73|- Mechanical compliance defects are caught before they reach the Evaluate
    74|  participant, allowing adversarial review to focus on architectural quality.
    75|- The gate is inert until activated — no tooling dependency at bootstrap.
    76|- Activation requires choosing a tool, which will produce a row in the
    77|  concern-to-tool mapping and potentially an adoption ADR.
    78|- The Evaluate participant's role is preserved: automated compliance is
    79|  a filter, not a replacement.
    80|
    81|---
    82|
    83|## Failure Criteria
    84|
    85|- The automated gate is activated before the manual lifecycle has been
    86|  exercised at least once — producing checks against untested assumptions.
    87|- Automated checks expand to cover architectural judgment, displacing the
    88|  Evaluate participant's adversarial role.
    89|- The gate becomes a bottleneck rather than an accelerant — indicating the
    90|  checks are too broad or too brittle.
    91|
    92|---
    93|
    94|## Definition of Done
    95|
    96|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
    97|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
    98|- [ ] The gate's position in the delivery lifecycle is stated relative to
    99|      ADR-007's phases
   100|- [ ] Activation trigger is explicit: first manual lifecycle completion
   101|- [ ] No tooling commitment is made in this ADR
   102|", "total_lines": 101, "file_size": 3883, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-009: Information Architecture Principles
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|As the framework matures, two failure modes become visible:
    16|
    17|**Structural accumulation.** Each new folder, artefact type, governance layer,
    18|or participant adds structural weight. Content accumulation in established
    19|artefacts is healthy — the glossary gains terms, the registry gains components.
    20|But structural accumulation — new categories of things — is the failure mode
    21|that makes governance feel heavy. Embedding a growing lookup table in an
    22|ADR body — the anti-pattern ADR-005 is designed to prevent — produces
    23|unnecessary supersession chains because the structure can't accommodate
    24|growth.
    25|
    26|**Information temperature mismatch.** The infrastructure manifest (ADR-006)
    27|demonstrated a second pattern: some facts change at operational speed (IP
    28|addresses, OS versions) while others change at governance speed (architectural
    29|roles, constraints). Mixing them in a single artefact couples their rates of
    30|change. The general form: artefacts that hold both "what you need right now"
    31|and "the full historical record" serve neither function well.
    32|
    33|These patterns are general enough to warrant standing principles.
    34|
    35|---
    36|
    37|## Decision
    38|
    39|Two principles are introduced to `principles.md`:
    40|
    41|### Resist structural accumulation
    42|
    43|Every new structural element (folder, artefact type, governance layer,
    44|participant) must justify its existence against the framework's complexity
    45|budget. The question is not "would this be useful?" but "does the value of
    46|this new structure exceed the cost of every future participant having to
    47|understand it?"
    48|
    49|Content accumulation in cold storage is healthy. Structural accumulation is
    50|the failure mode.
    51|
    52|### Cold for mass; hot for essence
    53|
    54|Hot artefacts hold only what the next action requires. Cold storage absorbs
    55|the full record. A cache draws from cold into hot for a session and releases
    56|on exit.
    57|
    58|Applied to the framework: Git history is cold (the full record). The current
    59|state of any artefact is hot (what the next session needs). Session wrap-ups
    60|and evaluation records are warm (referenced when relevant, not loaded by
    61|default). An artefact trying to serve both hot and cold functions will grow
    62|unwieldy — the resolution is asking which functions have better homes
    63|elsewhere.
    64|
    65|---
    66|
    67|## Options Considered
    68|
    69|### Leave these as informal heuristics
    70|
    71|Note the patterns in session records without committing them as principles.
    72|Rejected: both patterns recur frequently enough (three or more instances each
    73|in the reference implementation) to warrant standing constraints. Informal
    74|heuristics decay across sessions; committed principles persist.
    75|
    76|### Commit as standing principles via this ADR
    77|
    78|Chosen for: these are genuine constraints that ADRs and specs should be
    79|evaluated against, not just observations.
    80|
    81|---
    82|
    83|## Consequences
    84|
    85|- Future proposals for new artefact types, directories, or governance layers
    86|  must justify themselves against the structural accumulation principle.
    87|- Artefacts that mix fast-changing and slow-changing information are
    88|  identifiable as design smells.
    89|- The Evaluate participant gains two additional principles to evaluate against.
    90|- These principles apply retroactively as diagnostic lenses — existing
    91|  artefacts that violate them are candidates for restructuring, not
    92|  immediate defects.
    93|
    94|---
    95|
    96|## Failure Criteria
    97|
    98|- The principles are cited to block useful structural additions, becoming
    99|  a barrier rather than a filter.
   100|- "Resist structural accumulation" is interpreted as "never add structure"
   101|  rather than "justify every addition."
   102|- The cold/hot distinction is applied so rigidly that useful context is
   103|  excluded from hot artefacts, forcing participants to search cold storage
   104|  for routine information.
   105|
   106|---
   107|
   108|## Definition of Done
   109|
   110|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
   111|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
   112|- [ ] `principles.md` updated with both principles under the ADR-Introduced
   113|      section, referencing this ADR
   114|", "total_lines": 113, "file_size": 4261, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-010: Governance Dependency Sequencing
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|Every governance artefact rests on assumptions about concepts it does not
    16|itself govern. A component spec assumes a network fabric exists. A delivery
    17|contract assumes a lifecycle protocol exists. An adoption ADR assumes the
    18|concern-to-tool mapping model exists.
    19|
    20|When these assumptions are satisfied by committed artefacts, the dependency
    21|is explicit and verifiable. When they are not — when an artefact silently
    22|relies on an ungoverned concept — the dependency is invisible. Invisible
    23|dependencies compound: each new artefact that touches the ungoverned concept
    24|carries its own silent assumption, and the cost of eventually governing the
    25|concept grows with every artefact that assumed it.
    26|
    27|The infrastructure manifest (ADR-006) established the governance/implementation
    28|boundary — the first context where silent assumptions about instance facts
    29|became visible. The delivery lifecycle (ADR-007) created the first specs that
    30|could assume ungoverned upstream concepts. Both demonstrated the need for an
    31|explicit sequencing principle.
    32|
    33|---
    34|
    35|## Decision
    36|
    37|One principle is introduced to `principles.md`:
    38|
    39|### Govern the dependency first
    40|
    41|Every governance artefact rests on assumptions about concepts it does not
    42|itself govern. Each assumption must either:
    43|
    44|1. **Be governed** by an existing committed artefact — an ADR, a principle,
    45|   a glossary entry, or a spec that establishes the concept, or
    46|2. **Be declared explicitly** as acknowledged provisional debt — named in the
    47|   artefact with a reference to the ungoverned concept and a note that
    48|   governance is pending.
    49|
    50|Silent reliance on an ungoverned concept is a drafting error. The Evaluate
    51|participant should flag undeclared dependencies as blocking findings.
    52|
    53|**Cost inversion.** This principle creates a gravitational pull toward debt
    54|resolution. Each new artefact that touches an ungoverned concept must carry
    55|its own debt declaration, making deferral progressively more expensive.
    56|Resolution is bounded — one ADR closes the gap. This cost inversion is
    57|emergent, not designed, and is one of the framework's most powerful
    58|properties.
    59|
    60|---
    61|
    62|## Options Considered
    63|
    64|### Informal sequencing discipline
    65|
    66|Trust the Gardener and Generate participant to sequence artefacts correctly
    67|without a committed principle. Rejected: silent dependencies are invisible
    68|by definition — they are the class of error that discipline alone cannot
    69|catch. A committed principle gives the Evaluate participant an explicit
    70|criterion to flag them.
    71|
    72|### Committed sequencing principle
    73|
    74|Chosen for: makes invisible dependencies a detectable defect class, creates
    75|the cost-inversion dynamic that pulls toward debt resolution, and gives the
    76|Evaluate participant a clear evaluation criterion.
    77|
    78|---
    79|
    80|## Consequences
    81|
    82|- Every artefact must either satisfy its dependencies or declare them as
    83|  provisional debt. There is no third option.
    84|- The Evaluate participant gains a powerful evaluation criterion: "does this
    85|  artefact silently assume a concept that no committed artefact governs?"
    86|- Provisional debt declarations are visible in the governance record,
    87|  making the project's governance gaps an explicit, auditable inventory.
    88|- The cost-inversion dynamic means governance gaps become progressively
    89|  more expensive to defer — each new artefact touching the gap adds a
    90|  debt declaration.
    91|- Retroactive ADRs (for pre-governance components) are the resolution
    92|  mechanism: one ADR closes the gap and retires all associated debt
    93|  declarations.
    94|
    95|---
    96|
    97|## Failure Criteria
    98|
    99|- The principle is applied so rigidly that no artefact can be committed
   100|  until every transitive dependency is governed — producing deadlock rather
   101|  than sequenced progress.
   102|- Provisional debt declarations become permanent fixtures rather than
   103|  triggers for resolution.
   104|- The Evaluate participant flags dependencies that are genuinely out of
   105|  scope for the project, applying the principle beyond its useful boundary.
   106|
   107|---
   108|
   109|## Definition of Done
   110|
   111|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
   112|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
   113|- [ ] `principles.md` updated with the "Govern the dependency first"
   114|      principle under the ADR-Introduced section, referencing this ADR
   115|", "total_lines": 114, "file_size": 4536, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ADR-011: Seed Principle Amendment
     2|
     3|| Field             | Value        |
     4||-------------------|--------------|
     5|| **Status**        | Proposed     |
     6|| **Date**          | YYYY-MM-DD   |
     7|| **Author**        | [Gardener]   |
     8|| **Supersedes**    | —            |
     9|| **Superseded by** | —            |
    10|
    11|---
    12|
    13|## Context
    14|
    15|The founding `principles.md` includes the seed principle: both repositories
    16|are necessary to the project; only the implementation repository, the
    17|bootstrap secret, and the infrastructure are necessary to reconstruct the
    18|running system.
    19|
    20|This formulation is correct. However, the reasoning behind it — why
    21|reproducibility and evolvability are different properties, and why confusing
    22|them produces governance artefacts that over-specify operational dependencies
    23|— is not recorded in any committed artefact. A forker reading the principle
    24|cold has the conclusion but not the argument.
    25|
    26|Without this separation, governance artefacts tend to carry both "what you
    27|need to rebuild" and "what you need to improve" in the same documents —
    28|producing artefacts that are too operational for governance and too
    29|governance-heavy for operations.
    30|
    31|---
    32|
    33|## Decision
    34|
    35|This ADR records the rationale for the seed principle's formulation. It does
    36|not amend `principles.md` — the founding version already carries the correct
    37|text.
    38|
    39|**Reproducibility** is the implementation repository's property. Given the
    40|implementation repository, the bootstrap secret, and the infrastructure, the
    41|running system can be reconstructed. This is a reconstruction-level concern.
    42|
    43|**Evolvability** is the governance repository's property. Given the governance
    44|record, the next rebuild can be better than the last. Past mistakes prevent
    45|future ones. Architectural decisions compound forward. This is a project-level
    46|concern.
    47|
    48|**Both are necessary; they operate at different layers of time.** Any project
    49|that conflates "what do you need to run this" with "what do you need to
    50|improve this" will produce:
    51|
    52|- Governance artefacts that over-specify operational dependencies (because
    53|  they're trying to be sufficient for reconstruction)
    54|- Implementation artefacts that carry architectural reasoning (because
    55|  they're trying to be sufficient for evolution)
    56|
    57|The seed principle prevents this conflation by assigning each property to
    58|its correct repository.
    59|
    60|**For forkers.** If your project's founding `principles.md` uses the seed
    61|principle text from this framework's template, this ADR provides the
    62|reasoning. If you need to amend the seed principle for your domain, this ADR
    63|is the supersession target — amend the principle via a new ADR that
    64|supersedes this one.
    65|
    66|---
    67|
    68|## Options Considered
    69|
    70|### Omit this ADR if the principle is already correct
    71|
    72|The founding `principles.md` is correct; no amendment is needed. Argument
    73|for omission: an ADR with no delta is unusual. Rejected: the ADR's value
    74|is the reasoning record, not the delta. A forker encountering the seed
    75|principle needs the argument, not just the conclusion. An ADR chain that
    76|only records changes omits the rationale for the founding state.
    77|
    78|### Rationale-only ADR with no amendment to principles.md
    79|
    80|Chosen for: provides the reasoning record without an unnecessary edit to a
    81|document that already says the right thing.
    82|
    83|---
    84|
    85|## Consequences
    86|
    87|- The seed principle's rationale is committed and discoverable.
    88|- Future amendments to the seed principle supersede this ADR, preserving
    89|  the reasoning chain.
    90|- Forkers have an explicit argument for why the two-repository separation
    91|  is load-bearing, not just organisational.
    92|
    93|---
    94|
    95|## Failure Criteria
    96|
    97|- The distinction between reproducibility and evolvability is treated as
    98|  academic rather than operational — artefacts continue to mix
    99|  reconstruction-level and project-level concerns despite the principle.
   100|- This ADR is cited to prevent the governance repository from containing
   101|  any operational context, when the actual principle is about not
   102|  *over-specifying* operational dependencies.
   103|
   104|---
   105|
   106|## Definition of Done
   107|
   108|- [ ] Evaluation pass with no unresolved blocking findings
   109|- [ ] Gardener commit to the governance repository
   110|- [ ] `principles.md` confirmed to contain the correct seed principle
   111|      formulation (no edit required if founding text is correct)
   112|", "total_lines": 111, "file_size": 4267, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Stirps: A Governance Framework for AI-Participant Infrastructure
     2|
     3|**Stirps is a recursively self-modifying, memory-preserving, adversarially-evaluated institution for governing AI-participant infrastructure that produces functioning systems.**
     4|
     5|*This document is a seed. It transmits the minimum generative intent of the Stirps framework so that a reasoning agent — human or AI — could reconstruct the architecture from principles. It is not a specification, not a tutorial, and not a prompt. If you understand the why of each structural choice, you can rediscover the what.*
     6|
     7|---
     8|
     9|## The core thesis
    10|
    11|Build an institution that uses AI as participants, not an AI workflow that sometimes consults humans.
    12|
    13|The distinction matters because workflows optimise for output. Institutions optimise for continuity of reasoning across time, across participant replacement, and across failure. A workflow breaks when its orchestrator breaks. An institution survives the replacement of any single participant — including any AI model — because reasoning continuity lives in the governance record, not in any participant's memory.
    14|
    15|**The system remembers, not the AI.** This makes every AI participant a hot-swappable reasoning engine — commodity compute, not institutional knowledge. If a better model is released tomorrow, the Gardener swaps it in. The new participant reads the governance record, grasps the state of the institution, and picks up without loss. The framework commoditises the model deliberately; participant replaceability is a design goal, not a compromise.
    16|
    17|---
    18|
    19|## Why governance precedes execution
    20|
    21|The natural instinct is to build first and govern later. This fails silently: by the time you formalise the rules, the thing you built has already established conventions, implicit contracts, and undocumented assumptions that the governance layer must either ratify or fight. Governance becomes archaeology instead of architecture.
    22|
    23|The less visible cost is that implementation without intent generates implicit governance that is invisible, untestable, and fragile. Every ad-hoc configuration choice is an unrecorded architectural decision. Every workaround is an undocumented trade-off. Every firewall rule opened under implementation pressure is a commitment made without rationale. The debt is not deferred; it is incurred invisibly and compounds with every subsequent decision that assumes the ad-hoc choice was intentional. The result is a system that works but cannot explain why it works, cannot be confidently reproduced, and resists every future change because its actual contracts are buried in implementation details rather than stated in governance records.
    24|
    25|The framework inverts this: the governance layer exists before the thing it governs. Decisions are recorded before implementations satisfy them. Contracts are written before code fulfils them. The cost is patience at the decision stage. The empirical payoff is that execution becomes fast — not despite the governance overhead, but because of it. Decisions that have passed through the evaluation gate do not need to be relitigated, debugged, or untangled during implementation. Ad-hoc troubleshooting becomes negligible because the intent was clear before the work began.
    26|
    27|This does not mean governance precedes all *understanding*. Sometimes you must experiment to learn what a specification should contain. The framework accommodates this: exploration is legitimate, and its outputs can enter the governance record retroactively through supersession or adoption ADRs. But there is a sharp distinction between **experimentation** (deliberately scoped learning, acknowledged as pre-governance) and **implementation-by-iteration** (building the production system through trial and error and hoping the result is coherent). The framework governs the latter. It makes space for the former.
    28|
    29|---
    30|
    31|## The map and the territory
    32|
    33|The framework separates into two repositories:
    34|
    35|- **Gov** (the map): what the project intends to be, and why. Contains decisions, specifications, principles, and a machine-readable registry. Stable. Slow-moving. Append-only where it matters.
    36|- **Imp** (the territory): what the project currently is. Contains contracts, playbooks, configuration, and inventory. Fast-moving. Satisfies contracts defined in Gov; does not define them.
    37|
    38|The reference direction is asymmetric and non-negotiable: **territory points to map; map never contains territory.** An implementation artefact may reference a governance specification. A governance specification never references a specific implementation. Violating this collapses the map into the territory — the governance layer starts describing what exists rather than what should exist, and loses its ability to reason about change.
    39|
    40|Implementation experience routinely generates new understanding that the governance layer must absorb. When building a component reveals that a previously committed architectural decision was incomplete or wrong, the framework handles this through **supersession**: a new ADR is committed that supersedes the invalidated one, with rationale that includes the implementation discovery. The original record remains intact and traversable. Implementation learning is a legitimate and expected trigger for governance evolution — but the learning flows upward through a new decision record, never by silently editing the map to match the territory.
    41|
    42|---
    43|
    44|## Decisions as immutable records
    45|
    46|Architectural Decision Records (ADRs) are the fundamental governance primitive. Each records a decision, its rationale, the options that were considered, and the conditions under which it would be considered failed. ADRs are append-only: once committed, they are not edited. A decision can be superseded by a new ADR that references it, but the original record remains intact.
    47|
    48|This is not a convention — it is load-bearing. The reasoning history must be traversable. When a future participant asks "why does this work this way?", the answer is always a committed ADR with stated rationale, not an oral tradition or a comment in code.
    49|
    50|Key structural choices within the ADR system:
    51|- **Template as a separate file**, not embedded in the founding ADR
    52|- **Failure Criteria and Definition of Done** in every ADR from the start — these make evaluation possible
    53|- **Lifecycle stages** (Proposed → Accepted → Superseded/Deprecated) with explicit gates between them
    54|
    55|---
    56|
    57|## The human role: the Gardener
    58|
    59|One human holds sole commit authority across both repositories. This is not a bottleneck — it is a load-bearing architectural principle.
    60|
    61|The Gardener is the only participant who can make an irreversible state transition (a commit). AI participants generate, evaluate, coordinate, and observe — but they do not commit. This means:
    62|- Every committed artefact has passed through human judgment
    63|- No AI participant can unilaterally change the governance record
    64|- The rate of governance change is bounded by human attention, which is a feature, not a bug
    65|
    66|The Gardener's role is not to be the smartest participant. It is to be the one whose judgment converts proposals into commitments. The editorial function is the irreducible human contribution.
    67|
    68|---
    69|
    70|## Tempo and the session model
    71|
    72|Stirps is not an autonomous multi-agent system. AI participants do not run continuously, do not queue work for the Gardener, and do not produce output unsupervised. The operating rhythm is **session-based**: the Gardener initiates a session, AI participants reason within that session under the Gardener's direction, and the session ends when the Gardener commits or defers.
    73|
    74|This means the Gardener is never buried under a backlog of machine-speed proposals. The system moves at the pace of human attention and human judgment — deliberately. The constraint is not a limitation to be engineered around; it is the mechanism that prevents governance from outrunning understanding.
    75|
    76|Within a session, the tempo is conversational, not sequential. A proposal might be generated, evaluated, revised in light of the evaluation findings, re-evaluated, and committed — all within a single session. The generator participates in resolving evaluation findings, not just in producing the initial proposal. This is a dialogue between cognitive modes, not a pipeline.
    77|
    78|---
    79|
    80|## Four cognitive modes
    81|
    82|The framework identifies four distinct cognitive modes required for self-governing systems. These are more transferable than any specific participant architecture:
    83|
    84|1. **Generate** — reason forward from committed decisions to produce new architectural proposals. Participates in revising proposals in response to evaluation findings. Concerned with: what should be true next, and how do we get there from here?
    85|2. **Evaluate** — adversarially test proposals against the committed record. Concerned with: is this actually consistent with what we've already decided?
    86|3. **Coordinate** — translate architectural intent into executable delivery. Concerned with: how do we make what should be true become true?
    87|4. **Observe** — synthesise patterns across the accumulated record without participating in decisions. Concerned with: what are we learning about how we govern?
    88|
    89|These modes must not collapse into each other. A participant that both generates and evaluates its own proposals has no adversarial check. A participant that both coordinates and observes loses the distance needed for honest pattern recognition. The separation is the value.
    90|
    91|In the reference implementation, four AI participants occupy these modes (one each), mapped onto Stafford Beer's Viable System Model. But a simpler project might use fewer participants. The modes are the portable minimum; the participant count is an implementation choice.
    92|
    93|---
    94|
    95|## The evaluation gate
    96|
    97|Proposals do not become commitments without adversarial evaluation. The evaluator:
    98|- Tests proposals against the **specific committed record**: named ADRs, stated principles, registry entries, and existing specifications — not general impressions or stylistic preferences
    99|- Produces findings with citations to the specific artefacts that ground each finding: blocking (must be resolved), advisory (should be considered), or no-finding
   100|- Does not remediate — it surfaces problems. Resolution is a conversation between the generator (who revises) and the evaluator (who re-evaluates), with the Gardener holding final judgment
   101|- Operates with a conservative disposition: "be conservative, surface not decide"
   102|
   103|The evaluation cycle is typically: propose → evaluate → revise → re-evaluate → commit. The generator and evaluator are in dialogue, not in sequence. The Gardener observes, directs, and makes the final commitment decision.
   104|
   105|Because the evaluator cites against concrete committed artefacts rather than interpreting intent loosely, natural language ambiguity — while real — is a bounded problem. The governance record grows more precise over time as each ADR adds to the body of citable ground truth. The evaluator's job gets more constrained, not more ambiguous, as the record matures.
   106|
   107|The evaluator's independence is structurally necessary but fragile. Known failure modes:
   108|- **Predictability**: if the Gardener learns the evaluator's patterns, they can craft proposals that pass without genuine scrutiny. Structural mitigations: evaluator rotation, perturbation, decomposition.
   109|- **Capture**: if the evaluator is always the same model instance with accumulated context, it develops implicit relationships with the governance record that compromise adversarial distance.
   110|- **Context saturation**: as the governance record grows, the evaluator's ability to hold all committed ground truth in a single context degrades. This is a real constraint with known solution shapes — evaluator partitioning with overlapping coverage, machine-readable registries as structural indexes, hierarchical evaluation — but it is not yet fully solved. It is worth noting that this problem is not unique to AI participants; human reviewers face the same constraint at scale, and the mitigations (specialisation, checklists, structural aids) rhyme.
   111|
   112|These are mechanism design problems, not configuration problems. They require ongoing structural attention.
   113|
   114|---
   115|
   116|## The delivery lifecycle
   117|
   118|Governance produces intent. A delivery lifecycle converts intent into running infrastructure:
   119|
   120|1. **Spec** (Gov): what this component must do, written before any implementation
   121|2. **Contract** (Imp): how this component will be delivered, satisfying the spec, with concrete steps and verification criteria
   122|3. **Branch** (Imp): isolated implementation work
   123|4. **Gate** (Evaluation): does the implementation satisfy the contract?
   124|5. **Merge** (Gardener): commitment of the delivery to the main branch
   125|
   126|Each stage has a gate. Each gate has explicit pass/fail criteria. The coordinator translates between Gov-layer specs and Imp-layer contracts — it is a pure function (repos in, drafts out) that maintains no persistent state.
   127|
   128|---
   129|
   130|## Brownfield reality
   131|
   132|Any real deployment arrives onto terrain that already has tools, networks, and services. The framework handles this through a three-state model:
   133|- **governed**: component has a committed ADR and satisfies a specification
   134|- **pre_governance**: component predates the framework; acknowledged but not yet formally adopted
   135|- **pending**: component is in the process of being brought under governance
   136|
   137|`pre_governance` is an honest declaration, not a permanent excuse. Each pre-governance component should have a path toward governed status, even if the timeline is distant.
   138|
   139|---
   140|
   141|## What this document does not contain
   142|
   143|- **Participant constitutions**: how each AI participant should behave. These belong in the governance repo, not here. A cold session should discover participant roles from the committed record, not from this seed.
   144|- **Implementation specifics**: what tools, what hardware, what services. These are territory, and territory belongs in Imp.
   145|- **Behavioural instructions**: "be conservative" or "don't propose changes." These are governance artefacts subject to evaluation. Putting them here would create a shadow constitution outside the governance system.
   146|
   147|If the committed governance record is not sufficient for a new participant to orient themselves, that is a signal that the record needs improvement — not that this document needs expansion.
   148|
   149|---
   150|
   151|## The bootstrap sequence
   152|
   153|If you are starting a new project using this framework:
   154|
   155|1. Commit the decision to use ADRs (ADR-000), with a separate template file
   156|2. Commit principles — the standing constraints that ADRs will be evaluated against
   157|3. Define your glossary — canonical terms before any artefact uses them
   158|4. Commit structural ADRs in dependency order
   159|5. Formalise the ADR lifecycle — this should be the first ADR to pass through its own evaluation gate
   160|6. Initialise the evaluator — only after there is stable ground truth to evaluate against
   161|7. Declare all participants upfront, including dormant ones — retroactive declaration creates supersession chains and ambiguity about when roles actually began
   162|
   163|The bootstrap is the most governance-fragile phase. Decisions made before the evaluation gate exists cannot be adversarially tested. Acknowledge this debt explicitly and plan to address it. Note that this fragility diminishes with each instantiation of the framework — a well-written seed document reduces the distance between first commit and first evaluation, which means less unexamined debt accumulates.

   172|- Why is the Gardener's sole commit authority a feature and not a bottleneck?
   173|- What goes wrong when you collapse the generate and evaluate modes into one participant?
   174|- Why does governance that arrives after implementation become archaeology?
   175|- What is the difference between a workflow and an institution?
   176|
   177|If you can answer these from principle rather than by quoting this document, the seed has taken.
   178|

   180|
